articles.title: Advertising Board Licence (standalone billboard / LED)
articles.summary: An advertising board licence covers a standalone outdoor advertising structure — a billboard, hoarding, or permanent LED display — as opposed to the shopfront signboard that comes bundled into a business's composite premises licence. It is granted by the local council; the DBKL case in RAW is for a permanent LED billboard. The licence is annual, and the council charges a recurring yearly fee on top of the one-off application work. MISHU's documented service fee for arranging one is RM3,000, exclusive of the council fees.
articles.body:
# Advertising Board Licence (standalone billboard / LED)

**Status:** emerging
**Last updated:** 2026-06-23
**Sources:** [[MISHU Business License Services - Advertising Board (DBKL)]]

## Summary

An advertising board licence covers a standalone outdoor advertising structure — a billboard, hoarding, or permanent LED display — as opposed to the shopfront signboard that comes bundled into a business's composite premises licence. It is granted by the local council; the DBKL case in RAW is for a permanent LED billboard. The licence is annual, and the council charges a recurring yearly fee on top of the one-off application work. MISHU's documented service fee for arranging one is RM3,000, exclusive of the council fees.

## Body

### What this licence is, and how it differs from a signboard

Every business with premises needs a signboard licence for the sign over its own door — that is the advertising component of the [[fnb-composite-licence]] and is handled inside the composite application (the council articles cover the DBP-approved signboard rules in detail). An advertising board licence is a separate thing: it authorises a dedicated advertising structure such as a permanent LED billboard, typically displaying third-party or large-format advertising rather than identifying the occupier's own shop. The MISHU DBKL quotation scopes exactly this — "Item: Permanent LED Billboard" — and lists it as its own line, distinct from the composite signboard.

### Granting authority and scope of work

The licence is issued by the local council; in the documented case that is DBKL (Kuala Lumpur). MISHU's scope of work for the application, per the quotation, is: preparing and processing the documents, obtaining the checking report from all relevant government departments at DBKL, and delivering the licence. The quotation pairs the advertising board licence with a composite licence application, which is consistent with the structure needing to sit against a licensed premises or applicant.

### Fees

Two layers of cost, kept separate in the quotation:

- MISHU service fee: RM3,000 (excludes SST and any fees payable to the local council).
- Council fee: estimated RM3,000–5,000 per year, payable to the local council, varying by location and size of the board. This is a recurring annual charge, borne by the client.

Licence fees set by the government agency are decided later by the agency and borne by the client — the quotation flags them as a disbursement, not part of the service fee.

### Sales-scoping note

This is a single-source quotation (DBKL, dated 26 July 2022), so treat the figures as indicative for scoping rather than fixed. The council fee in particular is explicitly location- and size-dependent, so for any prospect the actual yearly cost has to be confirmed with the council once the board's dimensions and placement are known.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the composite licence carries the business's own signboard; the advertising board licence is the separate, standalone-structure equivalent. The quotation bundles a composite application alongside the billboard licence.
- [[mbpj-composite-requirements]], [[mbsj-composite-requirements]], [[mbsa-composite-requirements]], [[mps-selayang-composite-requirements]], [[mpkj-composite-requirements]] — council signboard and DBP-language rules that apply to advertising display generally.

## Open Questions

- The Malay-language verification requirement for advertisements (DBP / the root-level "TERMA DAN SYARAT PENGESAHAN BAHASA MELAYU DALAM IKLAN" document in RAW) almost certainly applies to billboard copy as well as shopfront signs — confirm and fold in on a future pass.
- No official council guideline for standalone advertising-structure licensing has been ingested yet (only the MISHU quotation). The DBKL advertising/billboard bylaw and fee schedule would let the article move from emerging to established and confirm the RM3,000–5,000/year band.
- Whether the advertising board licence can be held without an accompanying premises/composite licence, or must always sit against one, is not settled by the single source.

articles.title: F&B composite licence
articles.summary: An F&B premises in Malaysia operates under a composite licence issued by the local council, which bundles the business premises licence with the signboard (advertising) licence. The base document set is broadly consistent across councils, but each council varies in the exact copies required, submission method (online portal vs over-the-counter), and supporting approvals. F&B specifically adds health and fire requirements on top of the standard composite set — most notably a food-handler typhoid (TY6) injection and a food-handling course for the applicant and all staff, plus grease-trap and premise food-department checks.
articles.body:
# F&B composite licence

**Status:** emerging
**Last updated:** 2026-06-19
**Sources:** `RAW/Composite License Checklist.docx`, `RAW/F&B/QUOTATION/MISHU Business License Services - Restaurant(DBKL).docx`

## Summary

An F&B premises in Malaysia operates under a composite licence issued by the local council, which bundles the business premises licence with the signboard (advertising) licence. The base document set is broadly consistent across councils, but each council varies in the exact copies required, submission method (online portal vs over-the-counter), and supporting approvals. F&B specifically adds health and fire requirements on top of the standard composite set — most notably a food-handler typhoid (TY6) injection and a food-handling course for the applicant and all staff, plus grease-trap and premise food-department checks.

## Body

### What the composite licence covers

For a restaurant or food premises, the council composite licence combines two things into one application: the right to operate the business at the premises, and approval for the business signboard. The signboard wording must be approved by Dewan Bahasa dan Pustaka (DBP) for correct Malay grammar before the council will accept it — MISHU's business consultant handles the DBP application.

### Standard document set (common across councils)

Most councils ask for the same core documents, with only the number of copies and a few extras differing:

- Director's / owner's IC (or passport + visa for non-citizens)
- Passport-sized photographs of the applicant
- SSM company documents (Form 24 & Form 49, or Form A/B/D for sole proprietors and partnerships; MyData printout where required)
- Stamped tenancy agreement, sale-and-purchase agreement, or land grant / owner's permission
- Certificate of Fitness for Occupancy (CF) or Certificate of Completion and Compliance (CCC)
- Floor plan of the premises
- Location plan (Google Maps source is accepted)
- Current-year assessment tax (cukai taksiran / cukai pintu) bill
- Premises photographs — front, rear, interior, and side view if it is a corner lot
- Signboard visual with measurements, carrying DBP approval
- Fire-extinguisher receipt and a photo of the extinguisher mounted one metre from the floor (some councils)
- Temporary construction permit approval letter, if any partition or renovation has been done

### F&B-specific additions

On top of the standard set, food premises typically require:

- **Food-handler medical check including TY6 (typhoid) injection** for the applicant and all staff (explicitly required by MP Sepang; standard health-department practice for F&B).
- **Food-handling course certificate** for the applicant and all staff.
- **Health department / premise food department checks** — including grease-trap collection service.
- **Fire and Rescue Department (BOMBA)** support letter or inspection.

Where alcohol, live or recorded music, or halal certification apply, separate licences sit alongside the composite (see Related).

### Council variations observed

| Council | Submission | Notable specifics |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| DBKL (Kuala Lumpur) | Online via elesen.dbkl.gov.my | Account created with director's IC; ask client for existing account / password. Confirm office number, renovations, signboard lighting, business hours. |
| MBPJ (Petaling Jaya) | Online via elesen.mbpj.gov.my | DBP signboard approval valid one year only. Ask about signboard lighting and bank account details. |
| MBSJ (Subang Jaya) | Form-based | Copies must be crossed and marked "Urusan MBSJ sahaja". |
| MBSA (Shah Alam) | Over the counter | Prepare 4 copies; partition permit letter if applicable. |
| MP Kajang | Over the counter | Application form purchased at counter; needs real signature and company stamp; BOMBA letter collected from Hulu Langat branch. Process fee RM50 (business) / RM100 (industrial). |
| MP Sepang | Online and counter | Explicitly requires food-handler TY6 injection + food-handling course for applicant and all staff; entertainment form if any entertainment activity. |
| MP Selayang | Online (subject to portal availability) | Standard composite set; letter explaining why no signboard, if not applying for one. |

### Services and indicative fees (sales scoping reference)

A worked DBKL restaurant composite example breaks the engagement into these service lines (professional fees, exclusive of SST and agency/licence charges, which the client bears):

- Composite licence application — paperwork, processing, and checking report across government departments / local council
- DBP approval — RM850 (inclusive of printing and transport)
- Fire and Rescue Department — RM600
- Health department / premise food department (incl. grease-trap application) — RM600
- Indicative total MISHU service fee: RM2,050

This gives sales consultants a starting structure for an F&B fee proposal. Actual figures vary with case complexity and council, and licence fees set by each agency are borne by the client.

## Related

- [[liquor-licence]] — required where alcohol is served (to be compiled)
- [[music-licence]] — MACP / PPM / RPM, required where music is played in the premises
- [[halal-certificate]] — JAKIM halal certification; the premises licence and food-premises registration are prerequisites
- [[mbpj-composite-requirements]] — MBPJ (Petaling Jaya) council-specific detail
- [[mbsa-composite-requirements]] — MBSA (Shah Alam) council-specific detail
- [[mps-selayang-composite-requirements]] — MPS (Selayang) council-specific detail
- [[mpkj-composite-requirements]] — MPKj (Kajang) council-specific detail
- [[mbsj-composite-requirements]] — MBSJ (Subang Jaya) council-specific detail
- [[dbkl-composite-requirements]] — council-specific article (to be compiled)
- [[advertising-board-licence]] — the composite carries the business's own shopfront signboard; a standalone billboard / LED advertising structure needs its own advertising board licence

## Open Questions

- The health-department and BOMBA requirements are drawn from the DBKL quotation and the Sepang checklist; confirm the exact MOH food-premises requirements per council from the dedicated MOH/health guidelines once ingested.
- Food-handler TY6 and food-handling course: confirm whether every council enforces this for F&B, or only some.
- Liquor, music, and halal licences are referenced but not yet compiled — their requirements live in separate RAW folders awaiting a compile pass.
- Several other councils (Melaka, Bentong, Hulu Selangor, etc.) have forms in RAW but are not yet covered here.

articles.title: Halal Certificate (JAKIM Malaysia Halal Certification)
articles.summary: JAKIM halal certification is the Malaysian government's recognition that a product, premises, or service meets Islamic requirements across its whole supply and process chain. It is governed by the Manual Prosedur Pensijilan Halal Malaysia (Domestik) 2020 — the MPPHM — and the relevant Malaysian Standards (MS 1500:2019 for food, MS 2634:2019 for cosmetics, MS 2400 series for logistics, and others). MISHU does not certify directly; we refer clients to two halal consultancy partners (HQC and NAMI) who handle the documentation, training, and JAKIM audit. The consultant's job before referral is to confirm the client can actually meet the basic eligibility gate, then identify which of the nine certification schemes applies. This article is the pre-referral checklist: the universal requirements every applicant must satisfy, plus the category-specific requirements for the main schemes.
articles.body:
# Halal Certificate (JAKIM Malaysia Halal Certification)

**Status:** established
**Last updated:** 2026-06-20
**Sources:** `RAW/HALAL Certificate/NAMIxHALAL/HALAL/MPPHMDomestik2020.pdf`, `RAW/HALAL Certificate/Checklist Cover Documentation HQC (PREMISE).pdf`, `RAW/HALAL Certificate/Halal Quotation HQC Commerce 0401 - MISHU SDN BHD (29.06.2022) Get Halal License for F&B.pdf`, `RAW/HALAL Certificate/eBook Panduan Asas Halal Terkini.pdf`, `RAW/HALAL Certificate/my-halal-journey-partner-note.md`

## Summary

JAKIM halal certification is the Malaysian government's recognition that a product, premises, or service meets Islamic requirements across its whole supply and process chain. It is governed by the Manual Prosedur Pensijilan Halal Malaysia (Domestik) 2020 — the MPPHM — and the relevant Malaysian Standards (MS 1500:2019 for food, MS 2634:2019 for cosmetics, MS 2400 series for logistics, and others). MISHU does not certify directly; we refer clients to two halal consultancy partners (HQC and NAMI) who handle the documentation, training, and JAKIM audit. The consultant's job before referral is to confirm the client can actually meet the basic eligibility gate, then identify which of the nine certification schemes applies. This article is the pre-referral checklist: the universal requirements every applicant must satisfy, plus the category-specific requirements for the main schemes.

## Body

### MISHU's role and the two referral partners

MISHU advises and pre-screens; the halal application itself is run by a partner consultancy. Confirm the basics with the client, decide which scheme fits, then pass them to one of our two main partners. As a guide, the partner service fee for a halal certificate usually runs around RM5,000–RM7,000 (JAKIM processing fee and SST are separate).

On a worked 2022 quotation, MISHU itself quoted RM8,000 for a two-year JAKIM halal certification (F&B/retail), with a three-to-four-month timeline. That fee covers the full service — advisory and pre-screening, MYeHALAL registration and application, documentation development, hard-copy preparation for the applicant and JAKIM, and both the JAKIM desk and site audits — and bundles free HIAP awareness training for two staff (RM200 per extra person). The JAKIM processing fee (RM200 application + RM800 processing) and SST are excluded and borne by the client. The RM8,000 client-facing figure sits a little above the partner cost, which is the expected margin on a referred service.

- **HQC** — Halalan Quality Consultancy PLT / HQC Commerce Sdn Bhd, based in Bandar Baru Bangi, Selangor. A member of the HQC Group, with a strategic partnership with MIHA (Malaysia International Halal Academy) and JAKIM. Principal consultant: Muhamad Khairi bin Kamarudin, a Certified Halal Executive under JAKIM's Halal Professional Board. On the 2022 worked quotation, HQC charged RM7,000 (excl. SST) for a two-year product certification — covering advisory, MYeHALAL registration and application, documentation development, hard-copy preparation for applicant and JAKIM, and both the JAKIM desk audit and site audit. Free Halal Industry Awareness Program (HIAP) training for two staff was bundled. The JAKIM processing fee (see fees below) is excluded and borne by the client; 50% deposit before documentation, 50% on halal approval.
- **My Halal Journey** — operated by Ene Sumber Emas Legacy (003701913-T), based in Taman Serkam Ria, Melaka. Director: Hazni Abdul Aziz (mobile 018 294 0499; email myhalal_journey@yahoo.com; website www.sijil-halal.net). Provides JAKIM halal certification consultancy, and also handles two food-safety certifications that clients are often asked for alongside halal: GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice), indicatively around RM10,000, and HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points), indicatively around RM20,000. Those two figures are unconfirmed estimates — confirm with the partner before quoting a client.

### The basic eligibility gate (check this first, every time)

Under the MPPHM, every applicant — regardless of scheme — must hold:

1. A valid business registration from SSM (Suruhanjaya Syarikat Malaysia) — or a cooperative registration, or an establishment document under an Act of Parliament / ministry where relevant. The HQC checklist asks for SSM Form 9 and Form 49.
2. A valid licence or approval from the local authority (PBT), or a valid supporting/confirmation letter from a government agency. This is the council business premises licence — see [[fnb-composite-licence]].
3. A valid Food Premises Registration from BKKM (the food-premises registration under FoSIM Domestik), where relevant — applies to F&B products and premises.

On top of the paperwork, the applicant must be able to meet the substantive halal conditions:

- **Muslim Malaysian staff.** Permanent Muslim Malaysian-citizen workers must be employed in the processing area throughout operating hours or each shift. The number scales with industry size: large and medium industry need a minimum of two, plus a Halal Executive at each branch and an Internal Halal Committee; small and micro industry need a minimum of one, with a Halal Supervisor (small) and an internal halal control system. (For F&B premises this dovetails with the existing typhoid/TY6 and food-handling course requirements in [[fnb-composite-licence]].)
- **A halal management system.** Large/medium industry must build and run a Halal Assurance System (HAS); small/micro run an Internal Halal Control System (IHCS) — both based on the MHMS 2020 manual.
- **Halal ingredients and clean premises.** All ingredients must carry a valid halal certificate, or be supported by product specs, process flow chart, and MSDS. Premises must meet hygiene standards (pest control, food-handling discipline).
- **Prayer facilities.** A prayer room or space must be provided for Muslim workers, with prayer breaks allowed.

### What disqualifies an applicant (flag these before referral)

JAKIM will not certify, among others:

- Premises serving any non-halal menu, or hotels with a kitchen preparing pork-based menus.
- Business, product, menu, or brand names that reference or resemble non-halal items — ham, bak kut teh, bacon, beer, rum, hot dog, char siew and the like.
- Products or premises with negative religious or social implications: drugs, shisha, cigarettes, hair dye, nail polish, karaoke centres, entertainment outlets.
- Unprocessed, unpackaged natural products (fresh fish, vegetables, eggs).
- Finished products merely re-labelled in Malaysia without any local processing.
- R&D-stage products not yet ready to commercialise.

If any of these apply, the client is not certifiable as-is — raise it before passing them on.

### The nine certification schemes

The MPPHM opens certification to nine schemes. Identify the right one early, because the category-specific requirements and fees differ.

1. **Food and beverage products.** Must comply with MS 1500:2019 (Halal Food — General Requirements, 3rd revision), the Food Act 1983, Food Regulations 1985, and Food Hygiene Regulations 2009. This is the most common F&B scenario alongside food premises.
2. **Food premises** (restaurant, cafe, canteen, bakery, chain food premises, mobile premises, food-court cafeteria, kiosk). Certified per premises under MS 1500:2019. Hotels are certified per kitchen/restaurant. Catering, food-service, and convention central kitchens form a sub-category.
3. **Central kitchen (dapur berpusat).** A processing and preparation facility that supplies finished or semi-finished halal food to other premises — chain outlets and mobile premises. Treated under the food-premises catering schedule. This completes the F&B picture alongside [[fnb-composite-licence]].
4. **Contract manufacturing / OEM (pengilangan kontrak).** For companies manufacturing halal products on behalf of another brand owner. Requires the contract-manufacturing agreement letter. Foreign-company contract manufacturing carries a higher fee schedule.
5. **Cosmetics products.** Each product must be notified with NPRA (or hold a manufacturing-permission letter for export-only unnotified products). Processing premises must meet the Cosmetic GMP guideline (Annex I, Part 10 of the Guidelines for Control of Cosmetic Products in Malaysia) and MS 2634:2019 (Halal Cosmetics — General Requirements), plus the Control of Drugs and Cosmetics Regulations 1984. The NPRA Notification Detail(s) document is required.
6. **Pharmaceutical products.** Each product must be registered with the Drug Control Authority (PBKD); the premises must hold an NPRA manufacturer's licence and meet GMP. Eligible categories: scheduled-poison products, over-the-counter (non-scheduled) products, health supplements, and natural/traditional complementary medicines.
7. **Consumer goods (barang gunaan).** Finished products and/or goods used in connection with food, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, or medical devices. Governed by the MS 2200 series (e.g. MS 2200-2:2013 Islamic Consumer Goods — Part 2: Usage articles).
8. **Logistics services (perkhidmatan logistik).** Covers transportation, warehousing, and retailing in the halal supply chain (integrated logistics services). Governed by the MS 2400 series: MS 2400-1:2019 (Transportation), MS 2400-2:2019 (Warehousing), MS 2400-3:2019 (Retailing). The HQC checklist asks logistics applicants for their MS 2400 documentation — risk management plan, halal policy, halal committee, and SOPs.
9. **Slaughterhouse (rumah sembelihan).** Requires a Veterinary Health Certificate from the Department of Veterinary Services (JPV) and an appointment letter from the State Islamic Religious Department for the slaughtermen in the production area.

A tenth scheme, **medical device products**, also exists — each device needs a valid Medical Device Registration Certificate from the MDA (Medical Device Authority) under the Medical Device Act 2012 (Act 737), or an equivalent supporting letter.

### Documentation checklist (HQC, domestic application)

The partner will collect these; the consultant can pre-warn the client. Core documents apply to most schemes:

- Company registration (SSM Form 9 & 49); PBT business licence; FoSIM food-premises registration certificate.
- Company yearly income statement (P&L) — used to band the industry size.
- ICs of two local Muslim staff with offer letters.
- Valid halal certificates for ingredients, or product specs + process flow chart + MSDS.
- Product process flow chart, packaging/artwork, and a map of the premises/factory location.
- For product applications: list of products, brand names, manufacturer names. For premises/catering: the menu list.
- HAS / IHCS documentation.

Scheme-specific additions: OEM contract letter (OEM); NPRA manufacturing/import licence and product registration MAL number (pharmaceutical); NPRA Notification NOT number (cosmetic); MS 2400 halal documentation (logistics); JPV veterinary certificate and slaughterman appointment letter (slaughterhouse).

Held for the JAKIM inspection day: halal awareness course certificates, staff attendance records, invoices/delivery orders, typhoid vaccination and food-handling certificates, pest-control records, batch manufacturing records, production records, and the floor plan.

### Fees (JAKIM, borne by the client)

JAKIM's processing fee is set by category and industry size, and is separate from the partner's service fee:

- **Products and services**, per category per year, banded by annual turnover: RM100 (smaller), RM400, RM700, up to RM1,000 for large industry (turnover exceeding RM50 million). Categories: food & beverage, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, consumer goods, logistics, contract/OEM, medical devices, and central kitchen.
- **Food premises:** RM100 per premises per year (restaurant/cafe, canteen, bakery, chain premises, mobile premises, food-court cafeteria, kiosk).
- **Hotel kitchens:** RM500 per kitchen/restaurant per year for 4-star and above; RM200 for 3-star and below.
- **Catering / food service / convention central kitchen:** RM100 (turnover under RM500k), RM400 (RM500k–RM5m), RM700 (over RM5m) per year.
- **Menu addition:** RM200 for a two-year period.
- **Foreign-company contract manufacturing:** RM2,500 per application (ASEAN), RM10,000 (other countries).

All figures are the JAKIM fee only; the partner's service fee (e.g. HQC's RM7,000 product package) and SST are additional.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the PBT premises licence and food-premises registration are prerequisites for halal certification of an F&B business; food-handler and typhoid requirements overlap.
- [[liquor-licence]] — mutually exclusive in practice: premises serving alcohol cannot hold halal certification.
- [[music-licence]] — karaoke and entertainment outlets are among the activities JAKIM treats as having negative social implications and will not certify.

## Open Questions

- **My Halal Journey name card to come.** A fuller business card was to be supplied; the partner note in RAW captures the current details (director, contact, GMP/HACCP fees). Update when the card arrives, and confirm the GMP (~RM10,000) and HACCP (~RM20,000) figures, which are unverified estimates.
- **NAMI reference material remains useful, but NAMI is not a partner.** The `NAMIxHALAL/` folder in RAW came from a former MISHU staff member, not a referral partner. Its JAKIM materials (the MPPHM manual, MHMS 2020, and the scanned brochures) are legitimate halal reference sources and can stay — the MPPHM in particular is the backbone of this article. The scanned brochures (10–14 MB image PDFs) would need OCR if their content is ever wanted, but this is low priority since they are general JAKIM guidance, not partner-specific.
- **MISHU's own halal service scope** — resolved this pass: MISHU quotes RM8,000 for a 2-year JAKIM F&B/retail certification (3–4 month timeline), fulfilled via partner. Captured in the partners section above.
- **Standard editions.** The MPPHM in RAW is the 2020 domestic edition; JAKIM updates procedures periodically. Confirm there is no newer MPPHM before quoting fees or timelines to a client.
- **Certification timeline.** The MPPHM and HQC quotation don't state a firm end-to-end duration; HQC's deposit-forfeit clause runs at 6 months from commencement, which implies the documentation window. Log a real-case timeline as a team nuance once available.

articles.title: Import / Export Licensing (Customs, AP, and permits)
articles.summary: Importing or exporting goods in Malaysia runs through the Royal Malaysian Customs Department (JKDM) under the Customs Act 1967. There is no single "import/export licence"; instead a trader makes customs declarations and — for controlled goods — obtains an Approved Permit (AP) or agency permit before the goods can move. The decisive scoping question is whether the specific goods are free, controlled, or prohibited: that is answered by the Customs (Prohibition of Imports/Exports) Orders, which sort goods into schedules. Most goods clear with a declaration and duty payment; controlled goods need a permit from the relevant Permit Issuing Agency (often MITI), applied for through the ePermit system; and some goods are outright prohibited. Food and live-animal products carry an extra layer of DVS, MAQIS, and MOH approvals. The whole system is online — Dagang Net for declarations, ePermit for permits, ePayment for duties.
articles.body:
# Import / Export Licensing (Customs, AP, and permits)

**Status:** established
**Last updated:** 2026-06-22
**Sources:** `RAW/Import Export/Panduan Ringkas Prosedur Import .pdf`, `RAW/Import Export/Panduan Ringkas Prosedur Eksport .pdf`, `RAW/Import Export/ePermit MITI AP (Trader User).pdf`, `RAW/Import Export/Custom Prohibition of Imports Order/Customs-Prohibition-of-Imports-Order-2017.pdf`, `RAW/Import Export/Food & Meat Import Export/IMPORT & EXPORT MEAT & SOSEJ.pdf`, `RAW/Import Export/Food & Meat Import Export/MISHU Business License Services - Composite License (Import Food).docx`

## Summary

Importing or exporting goods in Malaysia runs through the Royal Malaysian Customs Department (JKDM) under the Customs Act 1967. There is no single "import/export licence"; instead a trader makes customs declarations and — for controlled goods — obtains an Approved Permit (AP) or agency permit before the goods can move. The decisive scoping question is whether the specific goods are free, controlled, or prohibited: that is answered by the Customs (Prohibition of Imports/Exports) Orders, which sort goods into schedules. Most goods clear with a declaration and duty payment; controlled goods need a permit from the relevant Permit Issuing Agency (often MITI), applied for through the ePermit system; and some goods are outright prohibited. Food and live-animal products carry an extra layer of DVS, MAQIS, and MOH approvals. The whole system is online — Dagang Net for declarations, ePermit for permits, ePayment for duties.

## Body

### The framework

Import means bringing goods into Malaysia by land, sea, or air; export means taking them out. Either way the trader must declare to JKDM and pay any duty or tax due — import duty, excise duty, and sales tax on imports; export duty and cess on exports where applicable. The system is built around the Customs Act 1967 and a set of subsidiary orders (the Customs Duties Order for tariffs, the Customs Duties Exemption Order, and the Prohibition of Imports/Exports Orders for control).

### The general procedure (import, nine steps)

1. **Customs declaration.** All imports must be declared, by an appointed customs agent, by AEO self-declaration, or by a registered Direct User.
2. **Goods classification.** Get the correct tariff (HS) code to know what duty/tax applies — via the HS Explorer, the Customs Technical Services classification branch, the Customs Duties Order, or the relevant Free Trade Agreement.
3. **Check if the goods are controlled or prohibited.** Confirm against the Customs (Prohibition of Imports) Order 2017 whether the goods are restricted, and whether an import permit / approval is needed from an Other Government Agency (OGA) or Permit Issuing Agency (PIA). Any permit must be obtained *before* importing.
4. **Declaration and documents for clearance.** Declare using Customs Form No. 1 (K1) for imports — K2 for exports — electronically via Dagang Net eDeclare, and submit the import permit (via ePermit) where required. Supporting documents: bill of lading / airway bill, invoice, packing list, country-of-origin certificate (if relevant), and any others requested.
5. **Inspection.** JKDM may physically inspect consignments.
6. **Duty / tax payment.** Pay via counter or ePayment.
7. **Customs approval** — granted once duty/tax is paid and any permit obtained.
8. **Customs release.**
9. **Keep records for seven years** in Malaysia.

The export procedure mirrors this: declare to JKDM (K2), classify the goods, check the Prohibition of Exports Order for control, obtain any export permit, then clear.

### Controlled goods and the Approved Permit (AP)

The gating concept is the Prohibition Orders. Under the Customs Act 1967 and the Customs (Prohibition of Imports & Exports) Order, the import or export of certain goods is prohibited *except* where an import/export licence — an Approved Permit (AP) — has been granted, subject to the conditions stated in it. Goods fall into schedules: absolutely prohibited; prohibited unless an AP/licence is held; and so on. Whether a client needs an AP depends entirely on the specific goods and their HS code, so that classification is the first thing to settle.

APs are applied for and managed through **ePermit**, a web-based system run on Dagang Net linking traders with the Permit Issuing Agencies (PIA). MITI is a common AP issuer (e.g. for certain machinery and vehicles); other goods route to other agencies. The trader submits the permit application online, the issuing agency approves or rejects it, and the status updates in ePermit; the approved permit is then submitted with the customs declaration.

### Customs ruling (Ketetapan Kastam)

Where the correct tariff classification or duty treatment of goods is uncertain, a trader can apply to JKDM for an advance customs ruling using the Borang Permohonan Ketetapan Kastam. This gives certainty on classification before committing to import. The RAW folder holds the application form and a checklist for this.

### Food, meat, and live-animal products — the extra layer

Food and animal products carry approvals beyond Customs. The agencies involved are DVS (Department of Veterinary Services), MAQIS (Malaysian Quarantine and Inspection Services), and MOH, alongside Dagang Net. For meat specifically:

- The foreign abattoir / processing plant (loji pemprosesan) must already be recognised by Malaysia. Check the DVS list of recognised foreign abattoirs and processing plants for the source country; if the plant isn't recognised, it must first be registered through the e-Borang Veterinar process.
- Once the plant is recognised, register on e-Dagang and apply for the import permit.
- For export of meat, follow the destination country's requirements and apply for the Veterinary Health Certificate (Sijil Kesihatan Veterinar, SKV / VHC) through the DVS export section.

Halal considerations apply to meat — see [[halal-certificate]] for the JAKIM recognition that underpins which foreign plants are acceptable.

### MISHU service fee (worked example)

MISHU service fees vary widely by what the case actually needs, which reinforces the point that the goods and the regulatory path determine the work:

- **Import/export permit assessment (MITI)** — examining the prohibition order and advising on permit requirements for a given HS code (e.g. 8429.51, heavy machinery): RM530. Advice/report only, not the permit itself.
- **Import/export permit (KPDNHEP, via BLESS 2.0)** — paperwork, processing, and delivery of the permit (e.g. HS 847990): RM2,000.
- **Food/meat import permit (Dagang Net, DVS, MAQIS, MOH)** — e.g. luncheon meat from Korea and pet food from Thailand: RM8,000 inclusive, about five months.
- **IILS freight forwarding licence (MIDA)** — for a freight-forwarding business: RM10,000 (normal price RM12,000), roughly 4–6 months.

So a simple permit assessment is a few hundred ringgit of service, while a food-import or MIDA freight-forwarding case runs into five figures. Settle the goods, the HS code, and the regulatory route before quoting.

## Related

- [[wrt-licence]] — an import/export trading company with foreign shareholding also needs WRT (distributive trade); the two are commonly scoped together for foreign-owned traders.
- [[halal-certificate]] — JAKIM recognition of foreign abattoirs/plants gates which meat products can be imported.
- [[liquor-licence]] — importing alcohol adds a customs excise layer (the RAW folder holds the Borang Kastam Lampiran A2 for liquor import) on top of the liquor licence.

## Open Questions

- **Freight forwarding (MIDA IILS) is its own regime.** The RM10,000 freight-forwarding quote points to a distinct MIDA licence (Integrated Logistics Services) worth a dedicated article — overlaps with the MIDA categories already in RAW.
- **This is a framework article.** Import/export spans several sub-regimes (general trading, food/meat via DVS-MAQIS, freight forwarding via MIDA, heavy machinery AP via MITI, plus the prohibition/duty orders). Each warrants a dedicated pass; this article is the spine they hang off.
- **AP issuer mapping.** A goods-to-PIA mapping (which agency issues the AP for which goods) would be the most useful next addition for fast scoping — build it from the Prohibition Orders schedules.
- "Custom Code Link.docx" is still cloud-only; sync if it holds a useful HS-code reference.

articles.title: JKM Pusat Jagaan Licence (care centres — older children, disabled, elderly, women)
articles.summary: A Pusat Jagaan is a care centre registered with the Department of Social Welfare (JKM) under the Care Centres Act 1993, distinct from the TASKA childcare scheme for children under four. It covers day or residential care for older children and teenagers (four and above), persons with disabilities (OKU), the elderly, and women, run by a private operator or an NGO. The single source compiled so far is the Selangor (PKMD Klang) registration document pack, which sets out the application and records checklists, the caregiver-to-resident ratios by category, and the floor-space calculation. Treat this as the Selangor baseline pending the national Care Centres Act guideline.
articles.body:
# JKM Pusat Jagaan Licence (care centres — older children, disabled, elderly, women)

**Status:** emerging
**Last updated:** 2026-06-22
**Sources:** `RAW/JKM License/Daycare Centre [JKM]/JKM SELANGOR CHECKLIST 2025/JKM Selangor.pdf`

## Summary

A Pusat Jagaan is a care centre registered with the Department of Social Welfare (JKM) under the Care Centres Act 1993, distinct from the TASKA childcare scheme for children under four. It covers day or residential care for older children and teenagers (four and above), persons with disabilities (OKU), the elderly, and women, run by a private operator or an NGO. The single source compiled so far is the Selangor (PKMD Klang) registration document pack, which sets out the application and records checklists, the caregiver-to-resident ratios by category, and the floor-space calculation. Treat this as the Selangor baseline pending the national Care Centres Act guideline.

## Body

### What it covers and how it differs from TASKA

The Selangor document pack registers a "Pusat Jagaan" by category: children (kanak-kanak), persons with disabilities (OKU), the elderly or aged (warga tua / warga emas), women (wanita), or mixed (pelbagai). Crucially, the ratio table states that children under four are not registered here — they are registered under the TASKA Act 1984 instead. So the line between the two JKM regimes is age and client group: under-fours go to TASKA; children aged four and above, the disabled, the elderly, and women go to Pusat Jagaan. A centre may be daily (harian) or residential (berkediaman), and management may be private (swasta) or NGO.

### Caregiver-to-resident ratios

The required minimum ratios in the Selangor pack are:

| Category | Residents : caregiver |
|----------|----------------------|
| Children under 4 | Register under TASKA Act 1984 (not here) |
| Children / teens 4 and above | 18 : 1 |
| Persons with disabilities (OKU) | 10 : 1 |
| Severely disabled / bedridden OKU | 4 : 1 |
| Elderly, independent | 18 : 1 |
| Elderly, bedridden / ill | 4 : 1 |
| Women / adult men | 18 : 1 |

### Floor-space calculation

Floor space is allocated per resident and varies by category: 3.5 square metres for children under 10, persons with disabilities, and bedridden elderly; 3.0 square metres for teenagers/adults and (independent) elderly. The maximum number of residents is the total activity floor area divided by the per-resident rate. The worked example in the pack: 145.75 square metres ÷ 3.5 = 41 children maximum, or ÷ 3.0 = 48 elderly maximum.

### Records to keep on site

The pack's records checklist (Bahagian B) requires staff personal files (personal details, proof of qualifications, skills, work experience, appointment letter), plus health, salary/allowance, duty-allocation, EPF and SOCSO contribution records; resident personal files (background, family/next-of-kin, problems and progress, birth certificate or IC, OKU card if any), plus a resident register, attendance records, property/savings records for residential centres, health records, and schooling records; and management records (visit/agency log, fire-drill and safety-training records, activity and incident log book, committee records, donation records). The centre must also display a signboard meeting the local authority's specification and display the Certificate of Registration, and keep the first-aid kit and fire extinguishers in working order.

### Welfare and safety conditions

The implementation checklist covers fire prevention (inspection by JBPM, building-evacuation and fire-drill practice with records kept, clear unobstructed exits, maintained working extinguishers) and health and welfare (toilets at a ratio of 1 per 5 residents, kept clean; sufficient water; separate sleeping, laundry, and bathing areas for male and female residents in residential centres; separate sleeping arrangements for married couples; clean, nutritious and sufficient food on an approved diet scale with the menu displayed; periodic medical checks and reporting of communicable disease; a sick bay; a maintained first-aid kit; and procedures for handling a death, including notifying next-of-kin). Food menus must be approved via the Ministry of Health nutrition portal (nutrition.moh.gov.my).

## Related

- [[jkm-taska-childcare-licence]] — the sibling JKM regime for children under four under the Child Care Centre Act 1984; the under-4 / 4-and-above boundary decides which licence applies.
- [[moe-licence]] — for school-age education the MOE/JPN route applies; Pusat Jagaan is welfare care, not education.

## Open Questions

- **Single-state, single-source.** Only the Selangor (PKMD Klang) pack is compiled. Obtain the national Care Centres Act 1993 guideline and a second state's pack to confirm the ratios, floor-space rates, fees, and certificate validity apply nationally, and to promote this article from emerging to established.
- **No fee, timeline, or certificate validity captured.** The Selangor pack is a document/records checklist; it does not state the registration fee, processing time, or how long the certificate lasts. Confirm these from the Act guideline.
- **Operator qualification and technical-agency support.** The TASKA scheme requires a KAAK/KAP-certified operator and JBPM/health/PBT support; confirm the equivalent operator-qualification and agency-approval requirements for Pusat Jagaan.
- **No MISHU service fee on record.** No MISHU quotation for Pusat Jagaan registration was found in RAW.

articles.title: JKM TASKA Licence (childcare centres — children under 4)
articles.summary: A TASKA (Taman Asuhan Kanak-Kanak) is a childcare centre that takes in four or more children under the age of four from more than one household, for a fee. Any such premises must register with the Department of Social Welfare (Jabatan Kebajikan Masyarakat, JKM) under the Child Care Centre Act 1984 [Act 308] and the Child Care Centre Regulations 2012 before it operates — running an unregistered TASKA is an offence under section 6 of the Act. Registration is made in person at the District Social Welfare Office (Pejabat Kebajikan Masyarakat Daerah, PKMD) for the area where the centre will operate, using Borang A. JKM processes a complete application within 21 working days, and the certificate is valid for five years. The scheme sits beside, but is separate from, the MOE kindergarten (tadika) licence for children aged four and above, and the JKM Pusat Jagaan licence for older children, the disabled, the elderly, and women.
articles.body:
# JKM TASKA Licence (childcare centres — children under 4)

**Status:** established
**Last updated:** 2026-06-22
**Sources:** `RAW/JKM License/TASKA/Panduan Permohonan Pendaftaran TASKA [FINAL].pdf`, `RAW/JKM License/TASKA/2) GARIS-PANDUAN-PENUBUHAN-TADIKA-DAN-TASKA.pdf`, `RAW/JKM License/TASKA/1) AKTA TAMAN ASUHAN KANAK-KANAK.pdf`, `RAW/JKM License/TASKA/3) Borang Baru eJKM.pdf`, `RAW/JKM License/TASKA/General Process.docx`, `RAW/JKM License/Daycare Centre [JKM]/Flow Process for Opening a Daycare Center.docx`

## Summary

A TASKA (Taman Asuhan Kanak-Kanak) is a childcare centre that takes in four or more children under the age of four from more than one household, for a fee. Any such premises must register with the Department of Social Welfare (Jabatan Kebajikan Masyarakat, JKM) under the Child Care Centre Act 1984 [Act 308] and the Child Care Centre Regulations 2012 before it operates — running an unregistered TASKA is an offence under section 6 of the Act. Registration is made in person at the District Social Welfare Office (Pejabat Kebajikan Masyarakat Daerah, PKMD) for the area where the centre will operate, using Borang A. JKM processes a complete application within 21 working days, and the certificate is valid for five years. The scheme sits beside, but is separate from, the MOE kindergarten (tadika) licence for children aged four and above, and the JKM Pusat Jagaan licence for older children, the disabled, the elderly, and women.

## Body

### What a TASKA is and who must register

Section 2 of Act 308 defines a TASKA as any premises that admits four or more children under the age of four, from more than one household, to be cared for in return for payment. Section 4 requires every such centre to be registered, and section 6 makes it an offence to run or take part in managing an unregistered TASKA. The operator may be a private business or a non-governmental organisation (NGO). The governing instruments are Act 308 itself, the Child Care Centre Regulations 2012, the compounding-of-offences regulations 2011, and the Director General's circulars — including circular 1 of 2015, which mandates CCTV in registered TASKAs.

### The four types of TASKA

Section 5 of the Act registers a TASKA under one of four types, and the type sets the floor-space rule and the registration fee:

- **TASKA di Rumah (home-based)** — admits 4 to 9 children, run in the operator's own residence.
- **TASKA Institusi (institutional)** — admits 10 or more children; set up by a private operator or NGO.
- **TASKA di Tempat Kerja (workplace)** — admits 10 or more children; set up by an employer for the welfare of staff.
- **TASKA Komuniti (community)** — admits 10 or more children in a lower-income area (household income under RM2,000 urban / under RM1,200 rural) and receives federal or state assistance.

### Who can be the operator

The operator must be a Malaysian citizen aged 18 or above who has passed the recognised childcare training course — either the Basic Child Care Course (Kursus Asas Asuhan Kanak-Kanak, KAAK) or the PERMATA Early Childhood Care and Education course (KAP). For a company, at least one director must meet that test; for a partnership, at least one partner; for a society, at least one office-bearer. The operator, manager, and supervisor must all hold the KAAK/KAP certificate. Caregivers (pengasuh) must be Malaysian citizens aged 18 or above and must pass the KAAK course within 12 months of starting work. The application form (Borang A) records caregivers as female only.

### Caregiver-to-child and floor-space ratios

JKM sets a minimum caregiver-to-child ratio by age, with a tighter ratio for children with disabilities (OKU):

| Age | Normal children | OKU children |
|-----|-----------------|--------------|
| Birth to 1 year | 1 : 3 | 2 : 3 |
| Over 1 to 3 years | 1 : 5 | 2 : 5 |
| Over 3 to 4 years | 1 : 10 | 3 : 10 |

Floor space per child is 2.5 square metres for a TASKA di Rumah and 3.5 square metres for an institutional, workplace, or community TASKA (the 3.5 figure includes shared spaces such as kitchen, store, and toilet).

### Minimum standards and technical-agency support

Beyond the operator, ratio, and space rules, the centre must register the business with SSM (or the Registrar of Societies for an NGO), display a balanced food menu and an age-appropriate daily activity schedule, hold suitable and sufficient furniture and equipment, keep the prescribed records on site, and maintain cleanliness and safety inside and outside the premises. Before JKM will register, the applicant must obtain support or approval from the technical agencies: the local authority (PBT), the Fire and Rescue Department (JBPM), the health department (Jabatan Kesihatan), and — in Sarawak only — the Land and Survey Department.

The fire requirements include emergency "EXIT" lighting and emergency lights on dual power supply (of a JBPM-approved type) and 2 × 6kg dry-powder extinguishers. The health requirements cover staff medical clearance (TB, HIV, communicable disease, mental health) and typhoid vaccination, a food-handler course, adequate lighting (over 200 lux) and natural ventilation (window opening over 10% of floor area), a no-smoking sign at the gate, no operation inside a factory building, an isolation area for communicable-disease cases, safe toilets and kitchen, a first-aid kit, and HFMD disinfection procedures. The health checklist is also used to monitor the centre every six months.

### How registration works

The process runs through the PKMD for the area where the centre will operate:

1. **Pre-registration.** Consult the authorised TASKA officer (Pegawai Diberi Kuasa, PDBK) at the nearest PKMD, collect Borang A (First Schedule, Regulation 4, Child Care Centre Regulations 2012) and the application checklist, register the business with SSM or ROS, and apply to the technical agencies for their support.
2. **New application.** Submit the completed Borang A with the full set of supporting documents to the PDBK at the PKMD.
3. **Inspection.** The PDBK inspects the premises.
4. **Fee.** Pay the registration fee once the application is checked and found complete (see below).
5. **Approval.** The PDBK processes the application; the PKMD officer endorses it if the JKM minimum standards are met; a complete application goes to the State Director of Social Welfare for approval.
6. **Certificate.** If the state office raises no query, the centre is registered and a Certificate of Registration is issued by the State Director, valid for five years. Any correction to the certificate must be notified in writing within 14 days.

Renewal follows the same procedure as a new application and should be started three months before the certificate expires. After registration, the PDBK monitors the centre at any time for ongoing compliance.

### Fees

The JKM registration guide states the registration fee as RM50 for a TASKA di Rumah and RM250 for institutional, workplace, and community TASKAs, payable by postal order, bank draft, or cash. The town-planning establishment guideline breaks the institutional figure down as RM300 for a new application (RM50 process fee + RM250 certificate) and RM290 on renewal (RM30 process + RM250 certificate). Read together, the RM250 is the certificate component and the process fee is added on top; quote the RM300 new-application total for an institutional TASKA. These JKM fees are separate from the planning-permission, building-plan, and business-premises licence fees the local authority charges where a change of use or new building is involved.

### The premises and planning dimension

The town-planning establishment guideline (issued by JPBD, the Federal Department of Town and Country Planning) treats TASKA and tadika together on the premises side. It sets four premises categories (special sites with or without a building already erected, and conversion of existing buildings) and, where a change of use or new construction is needed, routes the project through planning permission (Kebenaran Merancang) and building-plan approval before the local-authority business-premises licence is issued. For a straightforward home-based or existing-unit TASKA this planning step may not arise; for a purpose-built or converted centre it does, and it carries its own fees and timeline — much like the commercial-to-education premises conversion that arises for MOE centres.

### Practical sequencing and premises (team notes)

MISHU's working process flow sharpens the official steps with the order that actually works on the ground:

- **Settle the premises with PBT first.** Before committing, confirm with the local authority's building/planning unit whether the location will be approved. If it will, PBT changes the premises use (tukar guna) to "TASKA" — for example, converting a corner-lot residence. Only then rent or buy.
- **Engage a TASKA-familiar architect** for the renovation; they advise on the required safety facilities (emergency exits, fire equipment, OKU access). If the centre will admit a child with disabilities (OKU), build in OKU facilities — an OKU toilet, ramps, and so on — from the start.
- **Apply once renovation is about 80% complete** to PBT and Bomba; the JKM application can run in parallel rather than waiting.
- **Realistic timelines:** PBT and MOH take roughly two months; JKM takes the longest — expect six months or more. Plan the client's expectations around the JKM leg, not the council leg.
- **Premises quota by zone:** in a commercial area there is no quota, so a centre can open freely; in a residential area, check the availability/quota with the local council first, as residential approvals are capped.
- **Ownership:** the centre can be an Sdn Bhd with at least one local shareholder (foreigners are allowed as co-shareholders) or a sole proprietorship — but remember the operator/director test still requires a Malaysian citizen with the KAAK/KAP certificate, so foreign ownership does not remove the local-operator requirement. Note that a "daycare" taking older children runs to ages 0–12, beyond the TASKA 0–4 band, and is treated differently.

## Related

- [[moe-licence]] — the MOE kindergarten (tadika) licence is the sibling regime for children aged four and above; a TASKA (under 4) and a tadika (4+) may not operate at the same address. The premises planning route (Kebenaran Merancang, building plan, PBT and Bomba approval) is shared.
- [[jkm-pusat-jagaan-care-centre]] — the other JKM welfare licence, for care centres serving older children, the disabled, the elderly, and women under the Care Centres Act 1993.
- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the local-authority (PBT) business-premises licence and Bomba/health approvals overlap with the composite-licence premises process.

## Open Questions

- **Three checklist PDFs need OCR.** The standalone JKM Daycare folder holds scanned image PDFs with no extractable text: "2025 JKM CHECKLIST - USE THIS.pdf", "Senarai Semak - Jabatan Kebajikan Masyarakat.pdf", "Senarai Semak - Jabatan Bomba dan Penyelamat Malaysia.pdf", "Senarai Semak - Jabatan Kesihatan.pdf", "Borang Permohonan Jabatan Bomba.pdf", "1) Borang Permohonan JKM.pdf", and "JKM KUALA LUMPUR CHECKLIST DEC 2025/JKM KL UPDATED LIST.pdf". The fire and health requirements are captured here from the town-planning guideline's appendix, but OCR these to confirm the exact KL and current 2025 document lists.
- **No MISHU service fee on record.** The two process notes ("TASKA/General Process.docx" and "Flow Process for Opening a Daycare Center.docx") have now been compiled (folded into the practical-sequencing section above), but neither carries a service fee, and no MISHU quotation for TASKA registration was found in RAW. Confirm the service fee before scoping a TASKA proposal.
- **State variation.** The Selangor PKMD checklist (see [[jkm-pusat-jagaan-care-centre]]) and a separate KL list suggest document specifics vary by state office. Treat the JKM 2020 national guide as the baseline and verify the local PKMD list for the client's district.

articles.title: JTK Licence (Private Employment Agency — APS)
articles.summary: A JTK licence is what a recruitment agency needs to operate legally in Malaysia — a Private Employment Agency licence (Lesen Agensi Pekerjaan Swasta, APS) under the Private Employment Agencies Act 1981 (amended 2017), issued by the Labour Department (Jabatan Tenaga Kerja, JTK) under the Ministry of Human Resources. It comes in three types — A, B, and C — and the type sets both the scope (local placement only, versus overseas and foreign-worker placement) and the money: paid-up capital and a refundable money guarantee that rise sharply from Type A to Type C. The scoping job is to match the client's intended placements to the right type, then check the two requirements that trip applicants up most: a local individual must hold at least 51%, and the business address must be identical across every document.
articles.body:
# JTK Licence (Private Employment Agency — APS)

**Status:** established
**Last updated:** 2026-06-22
**Sources:** `RAW/Recruitment Agency [JTK]/1. SOP/JTK License [INTERNAL].pdf`, `RAW/Recruitment Agency [JTK]/FORMS/Senarai Semak Permohonan Baharu Lesen Agensi Pekerjaan Swasta (2022).pdf`, `RAW/Recruitment Agency [JTK]/FORMS/AKTA AGENSI PEKERJAAN SWASTA 1981 PINDAAN 2017.pdf`, `RAW/Recruitment Agency [JTK]/FORMS/JTK License (Agensi Pekerjaan Swasta).docx`, `RAW/Recruitment Agency [JTK]/Document.docx`, `RAW/Recruitment Agency [JTK]/QUOTATION/MISHU Business License Services - Composite & JTK License(MBPJ).docx`

## Summary

A JTK licence is what a recruitment agency needs to operate legally in Malaysia — a Private Employment Agency licence (Lesen Agensi Pekerjaan Swasta, APS) under the Private Employment Agencies Act 1981 (amended 2017), issued by the Labour Department (Jabatan Tenaga Kerja, JTK) under the Ministry of Human Resources. It comes in three types — A, B, and C — and the type sets both the scope (local placement only, versus overseas and foreign-worker placement) and the money: paid-up capital and a refundable money guarantee that rise sharply from Type A to Type C. The scoping job is to match the client's intended placements to the right type, then check the two requirements that trip applicants up most: a local individual must hold at least 51%, and the business address must be identical across every document.

## Body

### What the JTK licence is

The licence authorises a company to carry on business as a private employment agency — recruiting and placing workers (local or foreign) with another employer only. It is granted under the Private Employment Agencies Act 1981 (Akta Agensi Pekerjaan Swasta 1981, as amended in 2017), administered by JTK, with the licence approved by the Director General of Labour (Ketua Pengarah Tenaga Kerja). Applications go to the nearest Labour Office (Pejabat Tenaga Kerja) branch covering the agency's premises.

### The three licence types

The type is driven by where and whom the agency places:

- **Type A — local placement only.** Job placement for a job seeker within Malaysia. Paid-up capital RM50,000; money guarantee RM5,000.
- **Type B — overseas + foreign domestic workers.** Placement within and outside Malaysia, plus foreign domestic workers within Malaysia. Paid-up capital RM100,000; money guarantee RM100,000.
- **Type C — overseas + foreign employees.** Placement within and outside Malaysia, plus non-citizen employees within Malaysia. Paid-up capital RM250,000; money guarantee RM250,000.

The money guarantee is refundable and is paid either by bank draft or by bank guarantee (a template is held in RAW). Two practical rules on the money matter: the paid-up capital must be kept in the company's bank account (not spent down), and the money guarantee must be funded separately — it cannot be drawn from the paid-up capital. For MISHU's immigration clients, Type C (foreign employees) and Type B (foreign domestic workers) are the relevant ones; a purely local recruiter needs only Type A.

### Core requirements

- **Business description:** the company carries on business as a private employment agency to recruit and place workers to another employer only.
- **Company name:** must begin with "AGENSI PEKERJAAN" followed by the company name.
- **Shareholding:** a local individual must hold at least 51%. This is a hard floor — a fully foreign-owned recruitment agency is not licensable.
- **Premises:** must be a closed, private room with at least a table, a chair, and a bookshelf — not a shared open-plan or co-working space.
- **Business address consistency:** the address must tally across the bank statement, the local-authority (PBT) business licence, the SSM documents, and the tenancy agreement (with LHDN stamping).

### Documents (new application checklist, Form 1)

- Application form (Borang 1).
- Latest SSM company information (via SSM e-Info / MyData).
- Latest bank statement in the employment agency's name.
- Copy of the local-authority (PBT) licence, or a temporary/conditional approval letter from the PBT, in the agency's name.
- Shareholder details: IC/passport for an individual shareholder; latest SSM info / annual return for a corporate shareholder.
- Director details: IC/passport.
- Responsible director: IC plus a non-bankruptcy statement from the Malaysian Department of Insolvency (Jabatan Insolvensi).
- A statutory declaration (surat akuan bersumpah) before a commissioner for oaths.
- Processing fee RM300, by postal order or bank draft payable to "Ketua Pengarah Tenaga Kerja".

After approval, the licence fee and the money guarantee (both set by the licence type) are paid by bank draft to the same payee.

### Team-logged nuance — the address must match exactly

The single most common cause of friction is the business address. JTK places very high importance on the premises address matching across systems line by line: the address on the SSM record, the PBT licence, and Borang 1 must be identical. The official checklist states this outright ("ensure the business address on the SSM, PBT licence and Borang 1 are the same"). Before submitting, verify the address matches the local council's system exactly — a mismatch, even a formatting difference, holds up the application.

### Fees and timeline (MISHU)

On a worked MBPJ quotation for a company setting up as an agency (office premises), MISHU's service fee for the JTK licence (Type C / overseas + foreign employees) was RM3,000–RM6,000 (final figure confirmed with the business licence department), with a roughly 3-month timeline. That sat alongside the composite premises licence (RM850) and the fire certificate (RM600) for a total service fee of RM6,450. The government charges — RM300 processing fee, the licence fee, and the money guarantee — are separate and borne by the client.

## Related

- [[wrt-licence]] — both gate foreign participation, but note the JTK rule is stricter: a local individual must hold at least 51%, so a recruitment agency cannot be majority foreign-owned.
- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the agency still needs the council (PBT) premises licence, which feeds the JTK document set; the worked quotation bundles composite + JTK + fire.

## Open Questions

- **Confirm the final JTK service fee.** The quotation shows a RM3,000–RM6,000 range "to be confirmed by the business licence department" — capture the settled figure once known.
- **Licence fee schedule by type.** The internal note says the licence fee is "based on type after approval" but doesn't give the RM amounts per type — obtain the official fee schedule to complete the cost picture alongside the paid-up capital and money-guarantee figures.
- The two previously cloud-only docx files are now compiled: "Document.docx" added the capital-in-bank, separate-guarantee, and no-sharing-office rules; "JTK License (Agensi Pekerjaan Swasta).docx" is MISHU's SOP (v1.0, 12 Apr 2022) confirming the three types and a 2–3 month timeline. One conditions PDF ("SYARAT PERMOHONAN LESEN JTK.pdf") still returns no text (scanned) — OCR if it holds conditions beyond the checklist.

articles.title: Liquor licence (Lesen Minuman Keras)
articles.summary: The liquor licence (Lesen Minuman Keras, LMK) authorises a business to sell intoxicating liquor — retail, wholesale, or for consumption on premises at a restaurant, pub, bar, or café. It is issued under the Excise Act 1976 (Akta Eksais 1976) by the District Excise Licensing Board (Lembaga Pelesenan Eksais Daerah). The defining features of this licence are strict eligibility (applicant must be non-Muslim and at least 21), a mandatory public-notice process (newspaper advertisements in four languages plus an A0 display at the premises), and compulsory Customs and Police screening. Detail below reflects the DBKL (Kuala Lumpur) process; other councils follow the same Excise Act framework with local variations.
articles.body:
# Liquor licence (Lesen Minuman Keras)

**Status:** established
**Last updated:** 2026-06-19
**Sources:** `RAW/Liquor License/SOP/DBKL_GARIS PANDUAN PERMOHONAN LESEN MINUMAN KERAS.pdf`, `RAW/Liquor License/SOP/FAQ_JPPP_LMK_MY.pdf`, `RAW/Liquor License/SOP/MISHU Business License SOP Template_LIQUOR LICENSE.docx`, `RAW/Liquor License/SOP/3 – 6 Months Lead Time.docx`, `RAW/Liquor License/QUOTATION/MISHU Business License Services - Composite & Liquor (DBKL).docx`

## Summary

The liquor licence (Lesen Minuman Keras, LMK) authorises a business to sell intoxicating liquor — retail, wholesale, or for consumption on premises at a restaurant, pub, bar, or café. It is issued under the Excise Act 1976 (Akta Eksais 1976) by the District Excise Licensing Board (Lembaga Pelesenan Eksais Daerah). The defining features of this licence are strict eligibility (applicant must be non-Muslim and at least 21), a mandatory public-notice process (newspaper advertisements in four languages plus an A0 display at the premises), and compulsory Customs and Police screening. Detail below reflects the DBKL (Kuala Lumpur) process; other councils follow the same Excise Act framework with local variations.

## Body

### Eligibility

- The applicant must be **non-Muslim** and **aged 21 or above** on the application date.
- The applicant's name must appear in the SSM records, and must be one of: sole proprietor, a main partner, or a director of the relevant company.
- For the business itself: sole proprietors and partnerships must be non-Muslim owned; companies must have a **majority non-Muslim board of directors**.
- No paid-up capital is required, and any business entity can apply.

### Where liquor sales are allowed

- Permitted building types: hotels, commercial complexes, and shop-houses (rumah kedai).
- The premises must be at least **100 metres from residential areas**, and must not face a police station, place of worship, school, or hospital.
- On-premises serving (Rumah Awam) is allowed only at restaurants, eateries, pubs, bars, lounges, or club houses.
- Wholesale is allowed only at premises operating as a store/warehouse, in industrial areas/zones designated by the local authority and the Excise Act 1976.
- Retail is allowed only at supermarkets/hypermarkets. **Since 1 October 2021, no new liquor licences are issued to convenience stores, sundry shops, or Chinese medicine shops.**
- Liquor and drug stores under flats, apartments, or condominiums are not allowed; traditional-medicine and herbal shops under such buildings may not sell liquor.
- Temporary licences are issued for serving liquor at a specific event or activity.

### Application requirements (DBKL)

1. One copy of the business premises licence (the council business licence — see [[fnb-composite-licence]] for the composite that includes it).
2. One copy of the applicant's IC (21+).
3. One copy of SSM documents (Business Registration Certificate or M&A, plus Form 24 and Form 49).
4. Four passport-sized photographs of the applicant.
5. **Twelve newspaper-advertisement cuttings** of the Notis Permohonan Lesen Menjual Minuman Keras, published across **four local dailies** — one Malay, one English, one Chinese, one Tamil. Published three consecutive days, in the prescribed format. (The DBKL guideline states ad size 5cm x 9cm / 5cm x 3 columns; the FAQ states size A8, 5.2cm x 7.4cm — see Open Questions.)
6. **An A0-sized public notice** displayed at the front of the premises for seven consecutive days, in the prescribed format, stamped and confirmed by the Licensing Department (JPPP) in permanent ink before display, and monitored by JPPP.
7. Four premises photographs showing exterior, interior (different angles), and the liquor display area.
8. One location plan showing the proposed business premises (Google Maps source accepted).

### Process and timing

- Submission is **over the counter** only, and copies also go to the Customs Department and the Police Department.
- The applicant **must attend Customs and Police interviews/screening** when required, or within 14 days of submission to the central licence counter.
- New applications, transfers of ownership, and transfers of location must be submitted **no later than four weeks before** the Excise Licensing Board meeting; renewals no later than four weeks before expiry.
- **Estimated lead time: 3–6 months**, excluding the premises-licence lead time.

### Operating hours (by sale type)

| Sale type | Permitted hours |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Rumah Awam (pub / bar / lounge / restaurant) | 10:00am–12:00 midnight. Extension may be applied for in approved entertainment zones, up to a maximum of 2:00am. |
| Wholesale (borong) | 7:00am–9:00pm |
| Retail (runcit) | 7:00am–9:00pm |
| Temporary licence | As approved |

### Prohibitions

- No patron under 21 may drink liquor; a notice to this effect must be displayed at the entrance.
- No Muslim patron may drink liquor; a notice to this effect must be displayed at the entrance.
- Selling liquor in sachet form is prohibited.
- The manufacture, sale, and consumption of samsu is banned in the Federal Territory of Kuala Lumpur (effective 15 December 2020).

### Government licence fees (DBKL)

| Sale type | Fee |
|-----------|-----|
| Rumah Awam | RM110.00 per month |
| Wholesale | RM45.00 per month |
| Retail | RM35.00 per month |
| Temporary licence | RM30.00 per day |

These are government fees borne by the client, separate from MISHU's professional fee.

### Services (sales scoping reference)

A composite + liquor engagement (the worked DBKL example) breaks into these service lines: the composite licence application (paperwork, government-department checking, licence delivery), DBP signboard approval, and for the liquor licence — paperwork and approval-letter follow-up with the **Customs Department, Police (Balai Polis), Health Department, and Licensing Department**, plus the four-language newspaper advertisement and the Fire and Rescue Department. Professional fees exclude SST and agency/licence charges, which the client bears.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the composite premises licence that must be in place first; liquor is the most common F&B add-on
- [[music-licence]] — often applied for alongside liquor for pubs, bars, and lounges (to be compiled)
- [[dbkl-composite-requirements]] — DBKL council requirements (to be compiled)

## Open Questions

- **Newspaper ad size discrepancy:** the DBKL guideline states 5cm x 9cm (5cm x 3 columns), while the official FAQ states A8 (5.2cm x 7.4cm). Confirm the current required size with JPPP before advising a client.
- Fees and rules here are DBKL-specific. Capture the equivalents for MBPJ, MBSJ, and other councils as their liquor sources are ingested (the composite + liquor quotation references liquor paperwork at MBPJ).
- The Selangor/PDT excise route (RAW has a Petaling district excise form) differs from the DBKL route — compile separately and cross-reference.

articles.title: MBPJ composite requirements (Petaling Jaya)
articles.summary: Majlis Bandaraya Petaling Jaya (MBPJ) runs its composite premises-and-signboard licensing entirely through the eLesen online system at elesen.mbpj.gov.my, handled by the Jabatan Pelesenan (Licensing Department). An application moves through five steps — register, select activity, upload documents, save the acknowledgement slip, then pay and print the licence — with feedback promised within 24 working hours. The general document set mirrors the standard composite list, and every signboard illustration must clear Dewan Bahasa dan Pustaka (DBP) before submission. The licensing year ends on 31 December, so the renewal cycle and cancellations are tied to that date.
articles.body:
# MBPJ composite requirements (Petaling Jaya)

**Status:** emerging
**Last updated:** 2026-06-21
**Sources:** `RAW/Forms/Majlis Bandaraya Petaling Jaya/Panduan_permohonan_lesen_komposit_premis_dan_iklan_kemaskini MBPJ.pdf`, `RAW/Forms/Majlis Bandaraya Petaling Jaya/Notes for MBPJ.docx`

## Summary

Majlis Bandaraya Petaling Jaya (MBPJ) runs its composite premises-and-signboard licensing entirely through the eLesen online system at elesen.mbpj.gov.my, handled by the Jabatan Pelesenan (Licensing Department). An application moves through five steps — register, select activity, upload documents, save the acknowledgement slip, then pay and print the licence — with feedback promised within 24 working hours. The general document set mirrors the standard composite list, and every signboard illustration must clear Dewan Bahasa dan Pustaka (DBP) before submission. The licensing year ends on 31 December, so the renewal cycle and cancellations are tied to that date.

## Body

### Who can apply and what to prepare first

The online applicant must be a director, business owner, or company secretary registered with SSM, or a chair, secretary, or committee member registered with a relevant body such as the Registrar of Societies (ROS). Since October 2020 a company representative can also apply, provided an authorisation letter (surat penurunan kuasa) in MBPJ's format is attached.

Before first registration, have ready: a copy of the IC or passport (pdf/jpg), the SSM or business/professional-body registration document (pdf/jpg), a working email address, a mobile number that can receive SMS, a residential address, and — where the applicant is not a company director — the authorisation letter (pdf/png).

### The five steps

1. Register as a user on the eLesen system.
2. Search for the main activity, select or add the company name, and add further activities if the business runs more than one.
3. Upload documents. Each file is pdf up to 5MB, or jpg/png up to 2MB; multiple files can be uploaded at once. (The FAQ separately states a 2MB upload limit, so keep files small and split large ones.)
4. Generate and save the acknowledgement slip (slip akuan penerimaan), which carries the application serial number (EL-xxxxxxxxxxx) and a QR code for status tracking. The slip is not a licence and cannot be displayed at the business premises.
5. Once approved, pay and print the licence online.

### General documents required

The general document set for any activity is: a copy of the MyKad or passport; the key pages of the stamped tenancy or sale-and-purchase agreement; a floor-plan sketch with area measurements; a colour photo showing the activity inside the premises; a colour photo of the exterior with the adjacent premises; the SSM business or company registration certificate; the SSM owner or director information; and DBP confirmation for each advertisement illustration. DBP approval is obtained online first through dbpsahbahasa.my. Specific activities carry their own additional document requirements — MBPJ lists separate sheets for trade/business/industrial, food establishments, beauty and health-care centres, private parking, hotels, cyber centres, entertainment centres, and premises advertising.

### Payment, deposit and stickers

All licence payments and licence printing can be done online. Where an application carries a deposit (cagaran), the deposit bill is settled online via epay.mbpj.gov.my using the deposit account number (format Dxxxxxxxxxxxxx) found in the licence status menu. A premise sticker (pelekat) is issued by post or at the counter only for accounts carrying the RM25 administration charge; it is issued once and must be displayed permanently outside the premises.

Five licence types cannot be printed online and are handled separately: entertainment, cyber centre, hawker/night-market/Smart Trak, health-care centre, and outdoor (billboard) advertising.

### Application statuses

The portal shows the application as DRAF (being filled, editable), BARU (submitted), PROSES (received and under officer review, not editable), TIDAK LENGKAP (returned for missing documents), PRA KELULUSAN (approved pending site inspection — payment and printing of a temporary licence/acknowledgement card allowed), LULUS (inspected and passed at committee — permanent licence can be printed), TANGGUH (deferred for a stated reason, e.g. an unpaid parking or deposit bill), or BATAL (cancelled).

### Team-logged nuances

All MBPJ licence applications expire on 31 December, so the licence period runs to the year end regardless of when it was granted. To cancel a licence, draft an email to smartlesen@mbpj.gov.my. Cancellation of a BARU, PROSES, or PRA KELULUSAN application also has to be requested by email; only DRAF and TIDAK LENGKAP applications can be cancelled by the applicant directly. DBP signboard approval is valid for one year only.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the cross-council composite picture; this article is the MBPJ-specific detail
- [[mbsj-composite-requirements]] — neighbouring council, similar online model
- [[mbsa-composite-requirements]] — over-the-counter contrast
- [[liquor-licence]], [[music-licence]] — sit alongside the composite where alcohol or music apply

## Open Questions

- The two stated upload limits (5MB pdf in step 3 vs 2MB in the FAQ) differ — confirm the current ceiling with the Licensing Department.
- The activity-specific document sheets (food establishment, beauty/health, hotel, cyber, entertainment) are referenced in the guideline but their full contents are not in this RAW file — ingest them when available.
- `Borang Permohonan Pemeriksaan Pelan MBPJ.pdf` (plan-inspection form) is in RAW but not yet extracted.

articles.title: MBSA composite requirements (Shah Alam)
articles.summary: Majlis Bandaraya Shah Alam (MBSA) issues a composite premises-and-signboard licence and asks for documents in four copies, organised into three parts: general documents (Bahagian A), supporting approvals from outside agencies where relevant (Bahagian B), and the signboard set (Bahagian C). MBSA's checklist is unusually explicit about renovation permits — any partition or awning needs a separate Temporary Construction Permit handled at Wisma MBSA — and about the signboard, where the activity must appear in Malay at a font 5% larger than the company name. MBSA states that a front-of-premises signboard application can be processed and approved within one hour when the Bahagian C set is complete.
articles.body:
# MBSA composite requirements (Shah Alam)

**Status:** emerging
**Last updated:** 2026-06-21
**Sources:** `RAW/Forms/Majlis Bandaraya Shah Alam/Senarai semak MBSA.pdf`

## Summary

Majlis Bandaraya Shah Alam (MBSA) issues a composite premises-and-signboard licence and asks for documents in four copies, organised into three parts: general documents (Bahagian A), supporting approvals from outside agencies where relevant (Bahagian B), and the signboard set (Bahagian C).
