MBSA's checklist is unusually explicit about renovation permits — any partition or awning needs a separate Temporary Construction Permit handled at Wisma MBSA — and about the signboard, where the activity must appear in Malay at a font 5% larger than the company name. MBSA states that a front-of-premises signboard application can be processed and approved within one hour when the Bahagian C set is complete.

## Body

### Bahagian A — general documents (4 copies)

The general set is: the completed composite licence application form; the current assessment tax (cukai taksiran) receipt for the premises; an IC copy for citizens or passport-and-trade-visa details for non-citizens (the copy crossed through and marked "Urusan MBSA sahaja"); one passport-sized photo; full SSM company documents (Form 9, 49, 24 and the MAA, plus Form 13 for any name change) or full business documents (Form D, business info, owner info) as a CTC copy printed through the MyData system with QR code and SSM logo, or an association/club registration certificate; the CF or CCC; the tenancy or sale-and-purchase agreement or land grant for private land; a location plan; a floor plan; current premises photos (front, rear, interior, surrounding) with at least one colour copy; and the signboard visual with measurements in metres plus a photo of the proposed location and the DBP language-approval letter.

### Renovation permits (Bahagian A item 7)

MBSA singles out renovation. If the premises has a partition or amendment, the applicant must supply the Temporary Construction Permit approval letter. For a shop office or rumah kedai, the partition wall and awning need a Temporary Construction Permit using the Standard Plan, arranged at the Building Department counter on Floor 8 of Wisma MBSA. For a terrace, semi-detached, or detached factory, an additional plan goes to the One Stop Centre (OSC) on the LG floor of Wisma MBSA for partition, shed, or space additions to the front, side, or rear. If there is no amendment, the applicant writes "TB / Tidak Berkaitan".

### Bahagian B — outside-agency support (4 copies, where relevant)

Supporting approvals are required only when the activity calls for them: Department of Environment (JAS) for processing or storing hazardous materials; BOMBA for flammable or dangerous materials; KDN and Police for firearms; Police review for cyber café, used-goods (barang lusuh), or used-car activity; District Office liquor licence for selling or storing alcohol; FINAS or KPDNKK for video/VCD-DVD manufacture, storage, distribution or sale; landowner permission for activity on open land; Veterinary or Perhilitan approval for animal rearing; a document bundle for waste-collection industry (lorry grant, lorry photo, collection appointment letter, dumping location, payment receipt); JKM registration for a childcare centre (taska/pusat jagaan); State Education Department approval for a kindergarten, teaching centre or tuition centre; current Town Planning (KM) approval for tadika/taska/pusat jagaan; a worker name list with IC/passport/visa for food premises; and the food-handling card (TY2 injection plus food-handling course) for food premises.

### Bahagian C — signboard (processed within 1 hour when complete)

The signboard set requires the composite form, a copy of the MBSA composite licence, and a copy of the ROB (Form D) or ROC (Form 9 & 49, or 13) or society/clinic-doctor certificate. The activity must be stated in Malay, in a font 5% larger than the company name, and positioned above it. The sketch must show the activity, the SSM-registered company name, the company address, the advertisement size, the word size, and whether the sign is lit or unlit. DBP language confirmation is mandatory (call DBP or use dbpsahbahasa.my). The signboard must not block a tiered or glass structure, must not exceed the building roof, and the structure needs sign-off from a licensed or registered engineer; a large sign also needs a BOMBA review. Only one signboard unit is allowed. A two-face sign at the side of a building needs a neighbour consent letter (Borang Kebenaran Jiran). New or additional ads involving a clinic need a Malaysian Medical Council letter, and a tourism agency needs MOTAC approval.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the cross-council composite picture; this is the MBSA-specific detail
- [[mps-selayang-composite-requirements]], [[mbsj-composite-requirements]] — other Selangor councils, with differing signboard Malay-font rules (MBSA 5% larger; Selayang and MPSJ 30% larger)
- [[mpkj-composite-requirements]] — Selangor over-the-counter contrast

## Open Questions

- MBSA's signboard rule asks for Malay 5% larger than the company name; Selayang and Subang Jaya state 30% larger. Confirm whether this reflects different bylaws or a typo in one source.
- `MBSA_Borang Permohonan Lesen Komposit.pdf` (the application form itself) is in RAW but not yet extracted.

articles.title: MBSJ composite requirements (Subang Jaya)
articles.summary: Majlis Bandaraya Subang Jaya (MBSJ, the council for the MPSJ area) issues its general business-and-advertisement licence against the SM6B / Biasa checklist of eleven items. The identifying MBSJ quirk is that every document copy must be crossed through and marked "Urusan MBSJ sahaja". Signboards follow the MPSJ Advertising Bylaw 2007, which sets the national language at 30% larger than any other language and fixes the position of the activity wording and the licence number on the board. The applicant self-prints an acknowledgement account at the start, which points to an online intake step.
articles.body:
# MBSJ composite requirements (Subang Jaya)

**Status:** emerging
**Last updated:** 2026-06-21
**Sources:** `RAW/Forms/Majlis Bandaraya Subang Jaya/8a. SENARAI SEMAK UMUM (SM6B)(1).pdf`, `RAW/Forms/Majlis Bandaraya Subang Jaya/Checklist.docx`

## Summary

Majlis Bandaraya Subang Jaya (MBSJ, the council for the MPSJ area) issues its general business-and-advertisement licence against the SM6B / Biasa checklist of eleven items. The identifying MBSJ quirk is that every document copy must be crossed through and marked "Urusan MBSJ sahaja". Signboards follow the MPSJ Advertising Bylaw 2007, which sets the national language at 30% larger than any other language and fixes the position of the activity wording and the licence number on the board. The applicant self-prints an acknowledgement account at the start, which points to an online intake step.

## Body

### General documents (SM6B / Biasa checklist)

The eleven-item set is: the self-printed acknowledgement account (cetakan akaun penerimaan); one IC copy for citizens, or passport and Trade Visa details for non-citizens, with the copy crossed through and marked "Urusan MBSJ sahaja"; one passport-sized photo attached to the form; the SSM registration certificate; the SSM name-change documents where applicable; the CF or CCC; the stamped tenancy or sale-and-purchase agreement, or the land grant for private land; one location plan or a clearly marked Google Maps printout; one floor-plan sketch with the premises area noted; current premises photos (front, rear, interior, and side for a corner lot); and the signboard visual with measurements in square metres carrying DBP grammar approval, which is valid for one year only.

The SSM portion accepts several forms of proof: for a Sdn Bhd, Berhad or PLT, either Form 9/13, 24 and 49 with the MAA, or the Certificate of Incorporation with Particulars of Company and Particulars of Director, or the PLT registration with the List of Partners; for a sole proprietor, Form D (Kaedah 13) with the business and owner information.

### Signboard rules (MPSJ Advertising Bylaw 2007)

The national language must be 30% larger than any other language on the board. The premises activity is placed in the top-right corner of the advertisement, and the MPSJ reference number (the licence number) in the bottom-right corner. Language confirmation must be obtained from Dewan Bahasa dan Pustaka via dbp.gov.my before submission.

### Team-logged nuance

MBSJ insists that document copies are crossed and marked "Urusan MBSJ sahaja" — the same convention noted in the cross-council composite article. A shared MISHU working checklist (the English-language 14-item document list) sits in both the MBSJ and MP Sepang folders, so the two councils are processed off a common base set with council-specific overlays.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the cross-council composite picture; this is the MBSJ-specific detail
- [[mbpj-composite-requirements]] — neighbouring council, online model
- [[mbsa-composite-requirements]], [[mps-selayang-composite-requirements]], [[mpkj-composite-requirements]] — other Selangor councils

## Open Questions

- `Document.docx` in the MBSJ folder returned no extractable text — confirm whether it holds content or is a placeholder.
- The SM6B checklist is the "Biasa" (standard) category; MBSJ may run other categories (e.g. express/serta-merta) with different sets — ingest those if held.
- `Borang Permohonan Lesen Perniagaan & Iklan MBSJ.pdf` and `Borang Permohonan Permit Sementara Hiburan MBSJ - 2023.pdf` are in RAW but not yet extracted.

articles.title: MOE Licence (private education institutions — IPS)
articles.summary: An MOE licence is the registration a private education institution (Institut Pendidikan Swasta, IPS) needs to operate in Malaysia, granted by the Ministry of Education's Private Education Division and processed through the state JPN or district PPD under the Education Act 1996. The label covers several distinct centre types — tuition centre, enrichment centre, language centre, training centre, and kindergarten (tadika) — and the type drives almost everything: the company structure, the paid-up capital, the name format, who can sit on the board, and which students can enrol. The pre-screening job is to pin down which centre type the client actually wants, because a tuition centre and a language centre look similar to a layperson but have very different capital and ownership rules. A recurring practical hurdle across all types is converting the premises from commercial to education use, which needs an architect and council/Bomba approval.
articles.body:
# MOE Licence (private education institutions — IPS)

**Status:** established
**Last updated:** 2026-06-21
**Sources:** `RAW/MOE License/Enrichment Centre/MOE License - Education Centre Comparison Table.xlsx`, `RAW/MOE License/Kindergarten [MOE]/TADIKA SWASTA - Guidelines.pdf`, `RAW/MOE License/MOE Petaling Utama Tadika Checklist/LICENSE APPLICATION – ESTABLISHMENT OF A PRIVATE KINDERGARTEN (1).pdf`, `RAW/MOE License/Tuition Centre/MOE - New application form/PST 02 - Senarai Semak (1).pdf`, `RAW/MOE License/Enrichment Centre/QUOTATION/MISHU Business License Services - MOE.docx`

## Summary

An MOE licence is the registration a private education institution (Institut Pendidikan Swasta, IPS) needs to operate in Malaysia, granted by the Ministry of Education's Private Education Division and processed through the state JPN or district PPD under the Education Act 1996. The label covers several distinct centre types — tuition centre, enrichment centre, language centre, training centre, and kindergarten (tadika) — and the type drives almost everything: the company structure, the paid-up capital, the name format, who can sit on the board, and which students can enrol. The pre-screening job is to pin down which centre type the client actually wants, because a tuition centre and a language centre look similar to a layperson but have very different capital and ownership rules. A recurring practical hurdle across all types is converting the premises from commercial to education use, which needs an architect and council/Bomba approval.

## Body

### What the MOE licence is

Private education in Malaysia runs under the Education Act 1996. A centre registers as an Institut Pendidikan Swasta (IPS) with the Ministry of Education (KPM) through the Bahagian Pendidikan Swasta, handled on the ground by the state education department (JPN) or district education office (PPD). The registration is centre-specific: the company must show that education is one of its registered activities, name the centre to a prescribed format, meet a paid-up capital floor, and appoint a governance structure (chairman, board of governors, principal, teachers) before the premises is inspected and the licence issued.

### The centre types at a glance

The single most useful tool for scoping is the type comparison. The four main non-kindergarten types differ as follows:

- **Tuition centre (Pusat Tuisyen)** — teaches the local school syllabus only (KSSR / KSSM), students aged 7 to 17. Can be an enterprise, PLT, or Sdn Bhd. Name must start with "PUSAT TUISYEN". Paid-up capital RM10,000. Board of governors of 3 (local only). The lightest-weight type.
- **Enrichment centre (Pusat Perkembangan Minda)** — cognitive-development programmes using specific methodologies (tools, musical instruments, calculation or writing techniques); supplementary to formal schooling, and the certificate is for attendance only. Students aged 7 and above. Sdn Bhd only. Name must start with "PUSAT PERKEMBANGAN MINDA". Paid-up capital RM100,000 (KL) or RM50,000 (Selangor). Board of 5 (local only). The SSM company name must itself signal education (e.g. Smart, Learning, Education).
- **Language centre (Pusat Bahasa)** — languages only (e.g. Japanese, French, English), students aged 7 and above. Sdn Bhd only. Name must start with "PUSAT BAHASA". Paid-up capital RM100,000. Board of 5 — and uniquely, foreigners are allowed on the board and as teachers. The premises must be a commercial unit with a layout plan approved by PBT and Bomba (needs an architect).
- **Training centre (Pusat Latihan)** — technical fields (IT, business, accounting and similar); short certificate courses not exceeding 3 months or 20 credit hours per level. Malaysian students 16 and above; international students 18 to 45. Sdn Bhd only. Name must start with "PUSAT LATIHAN". Paid-up capital RM50,000. Board of 5 (local only).

Across all four: the shareholding rule is 30% Bumiputera / 70% local or foreign; the chairman is one local person who cannot be a board member; the principal is one local person who cannot be a board member; and a minimum of one teacher is required (local only, except the language centre where a foreigner is allowed).

### Foreign equity by nationality

Foreign ownership in education is governed by each country's Free Trade Agreement with Malaysia, so the cap varies by the investor's nationality. The worked figures captured for the language centre are: Spain, India, and the United States up to 20%; China, Japan, and Korea up to 49%; Singapore and Indonesia up to 51%. Confirm the current cap for the client's nationality before advising, and note this links to immigration work — a language centre is the one type that can place foreigners on the board and in teaching roles, which feeds Employment Pass planning.

### Kindergarten (Tadika) — its own rules

A private kindergarten is a separate scheme under the Private Education Division, set out in the 2021 Garis Panduan Penubuhan Tadika Swasta. Key points:

- **Ownership:** must be owned and operated by Malaysian citizens; if incorporated under ROC, minimum 30% Bumiputera equity.
- **Premises:** allowed in government-office premises, single or multi-storey terrace/bungalow houses, or dedicated kindergarten space — subject to the multi-storey building guideline (up to about level 5 / 18 metres) and PBT approval. A kindergarten in a residential area needs a written permission letter from the residents' association / neighbourhood. Tadika (kindergarten) and Taska (nursery) may not operate at the same address, though they may sit in adjacent premises.
- **Principal:** must hold a diploma in Early Childhood Education or equivalent qualification/experience.
- **Management:** at least two persons (ahli pengelola).
- **Operations:** maximum learning time 4 hours, ending by about 5:30 p.m.; maximum fees not exceeding RM150.
- **Process and timeline:** name checked with PPD and SSM first; online registration plus two printed ring-file sets submitted in person to the PPD; premises inspection; presentation to a review panel; licence issued. The PPD sets a small processing fee (e.g. RM15 for three years). The whole process takes roughly 6 to 9 months.

### Common documents (tuition centre checklist as the model)

The application checklists share a common spine. For a tuition centre the Private Education Division asks for:

- The establishment form (Borang BPS-1) and a covering application letter.
- A certified copy of the applicant's IC.
- SSM documents: for an Sdn Bhd, the latest Section 14 and Section 51, the registration certificate, particulars/summary of share capital, summary of shareholders, particulars of directors, and the M&A or practice directive stating education as a company activity. For a sole proprietor/partnership/PLT, the business-name approval (PNA.42). For a society/association/foundation/cooperative, the ROS registration and constitution showing education as an activity.
- Proof of financial position: bank statement in the centre's name showing the required paid-up capital (RM10,000 for a tuition centre, with 30% Bumiputera equity for an Sdn Bhd).
- Governance appointments — chairman of the board of governors, board members, principal, and teachers — each with appointment letter, IC, and academic qualifications.

### The premises conversion hurdle

A point worth raising early with any MOE client: the premises usually needs its status converting from commercial to education use, which runs through the town planning department (Jabatan Perancang Bandar) and the building department (Jabatan Bangunan) and requires an architect to prepare and submit the layout plan (and Bomba approval). This is a separate cost and timeline from the licence itself.

### MISHU service fee

On the worked quotation, MISHU charged RM7,000 (excl. SST) for the MOE licence service — paperwork, processing, and checking with the Private Education Division. The architectural work to convert commercial premises to education use is quoted separately at RM5,000–RM10,000, set by the architect, covering the town planning and building department submissions. JAKIM-style agency fees and disbursements are borne by the client.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the council (PBT) premises approval and the commercial-to-education conversion overlap with the composite-licence premises process.
- [[wrt-licence]] — for a foreign-owned education centre, the FTA-based equity caps here sit alongside the WRT/USS foreign-participation regime; a language centre with foreign board members and teachers also feeds Employment Pass planning.
- [[jkm-taska-childcare-licence]] — for childcare under age 4 the JKM TASKA licence applies instead of (or alongside) the MOE tadika licence; a TASKA and a tadika may not share the same address, and both share the premises planning route.

## Open Questions

- **Two checklists need OCR.** "Enrichment Centre Checklist.pdf" and "Language Center/MOE KL- Checklist.pdf" are scanned image PDFs with no extractable text. OCR them to capture the enrichment and language centre document lists at the same level of detail as the tuition centre checklist.
- **Capital figure by state.** The enrichment centre paid-up capital differs by state (RM100,000 KL / RM50,000 Selangor). Confirm whether other states follow the KL or Selangor figure before quoting.
- **Confirm the kindergarten age range.** The comparison table is silent on tadika ages; the standard tadika range is 4–6, but verify against the 2021 guideline's definition section, which wasn't fully extracted this pass.
- Not yet extracted: the BPS-1 establishment forms and several SOP/letter templates (some still cloud-only). The requirements are covered by the guidelines and checklists; the forms are fillable templates rather than new requirements.

articles.title: MOTAC Tourism Licence (TOBTAB + tourist accommodation)
articles.summary: A MOTAC licence lets a company run a tourism business in Malaysia under the Tourism Industry Act 1992 [Act 482], administered by the Ministry of Tourism, Arts and Culture and applied for through the TOURLIST portal. The main licence is TOBTAB — Tour Operating Business and Travel Agency Business — which splits into domestic (inbound) tour operating, outbound tour operating, and travel agency (ticketing), with an Umrah/Ziarah add-on. The decisive factor for scoping, especially for foreign clients, is the equity-and-capital table: a wholly Malaysian-owned travel agency needs RM200,000 paid-up, but a foreign-owned tour operator can need up to RM1.5 million, and outbound tour operating is closed to foreign companies entirely. Tourist accommodation (hotels) is a separate MOTAC registration, classified into City, Resort, Highland, Boutique, and Innovative. The pre-screening job is to fix the exact licence sub-type and the shareholders' nationality, then read the paid-up capital straight off Appendix A.
articles.body:
# MOTAC Tourism Licence (TOBTAB + tourist accommodation)

**Status:** established
**Last updated:** 2026-06-21
**Sources:** `RAW/Tourism [MOTAC]/1. SOP/1) Travel Agency/TOBTAB Guidelines_English version.pdf`, `RAW/Tourism [MOTAC]/1. SOP/1) Travel Agency/Garis Panduan Permohonan Lesen Perniagaan Pengendalian Pelancongan Dan Agensi Pengembaraan TOBTAB.pdf`, `RAW/Tourism [MOTAC]/1. SOP/1) Travel Agency/MOTAC License [INTERNAL].pdf`, `RAW/Tourism [MOTAC]/1. SOP/2) Tourist Accommodation/GARIS PANDUAN PENGELASAN PPP [English Version].pdf`, `RAW/Tourism [MOTAC]/motac-foreign-director-ep-nuance-note.md`

## Summary

A MOTAC licence lets a company run a tourism business in Malaysia under the Tourism Industry Act 1992 [Act 482], administered by the Ministry of Tourism, Arts and Culture and applied for through the TOURLIST portal. The main licence is TOBTAB — Tour Operating Business and Travel Agency Business — which splits into domestic (inbound) tour operating, outbound tour operating, and travel agency (ticketing), with an Umrah/Ziarah add-on. The decisive factor for scoping, especially for foreign clients, is the equity-and-capital table: a wholly Malaysian-owned travel agency needs RM200,000 paid-up, but a foreign-owned tour operator can need up to RM1.5 million, and outbound tour operating is closed to foreign companies entirely. Tourist accommodation (hotels) is a separate MOTAC registration, classified into City, Resort, Highland, Boutique, and Innovative. The pre-screening job is to fix the exact licence sub-type and the shareholders' nationality, then read the paid-up capital straight off Appendix A.

## Body

### What the MOTAC licence is

Tourism businesses are licensed under the Tourism Industry Act 1992 [Act 482]. The applicant must be a private limited company (Sdn Bhd) incorporated under the Companies Act 2016, with "Nature of Business" in its SSM e-Info stating tour operating and/or travel agency, and a company name that reflects tourism or travel. Applications run entirely through the TOURLIST system (www.tourlist.gov.my). The current guideline is effective from 1 August 2024.

### The TOBTAB licence types

- **Domestic tour operating (Pengendalian Pelancongan Dalam Negeri)** — inbound tours within Malaysia.
- **Outbound tour operating (Pengendalian Pelancongan Luar Negeri)** — tours outside Malaysia. An Umrah/Ziarah scope can be added.
- **Travel agency (Agensi Pengembaraan)** — ticketing and travel agency services.

These can be held singly or combined. MISHU's internal framing lists the practical types as inbound, outbound, ticketing, and travel accommodation.

### Equity and paid-up capital — read this off Appendix A

This is the heart of the scoping decision. The required paid-up capital depends on the licence type, whether the shareholder is local or foreign, the foreign shareholder's origin (ASEAN vs non-ASEAN), and whether the premises is urban (Majlis Bandaraya/Perbandaran area) or rural (Majlis Daerah area). The key cases:

Foreign-owned companies:

- Domestic tour operating, foreign company (ASEAN or non-ASEAN): up to 100% foreign equity allowed, but paid-up capital RM1,500,000.
- Domestic tour operating + travel agency, ASEAN foreign company: 70% equity (Singapore/Cambodia only) or 51% (other than Singapore/Cambodia); paid-up RM1,000,000.
- Domestic tour operating + travel agency, non-ASEAN foreign company: 30% equity; paid-up RM500,000.
- Outbound tour operating, any foreign company: **not allowed**.
- A foreign company holding more than 51% equity cannot hold the licence, except the wholly-foreign domestic-only and 70% Singapore/Cambodia cases above.

Wholly Malaysian-owned (citizen or local company, 100%):

- Domestic tour operating: RM200,000 (urban) / RM50,000 (rural).
- Outbound tour operating: RM200,000.
- Travel agency: RM200,000 (urban) / RM100,000 (rural).
- Domestic + outbound: RM200,000.
- Domestic + travel agency: RM200,000 (urban) / RM150,000 (rural).
- Outbound + travel agency: RM200,000.
- Domestic + outbound + travel agency: RM200,000.

A reduced RM50,000 paid-up applies for domestic-only tour operating in Labuan, Langkawi, Perlis, and the Pahang resort islands, but those companies may not open branches elsewhere.

Two further conditions matter for foreign clients: foreign shareholders must have at least three years of tourism experience, and shareholders must not hold equity in any other tourism company licensed under Act 482. MISHU's internal rule of thumb for a clean application is a local shareholder holding 100%, paid-up capital around RM250,000, and — where foreign participation is needed — only foreign corporate shareholders, at a permitted percentage with the correspondingly higher paid-up capital.

### Required documents

- SSM e-Info, printed no more than 14 days before the application.
- MyKad (Malaysian) or passport (non-Malaysian) for all directors and shareholders; employment pass for foreign directors.
- For a foreign company shareholder: a certified copy of the company registration certificate, certified by Tourism Malaysia, the Malaysian Embassy, or the High Commission in the relevant country.
- Director's Letter of Undertaking (Form BA/2).
- PERKESO (SOCSO) registration confirmation.
- Banking information (front page of company account statements) and a director's resolution for each company account.
- For an outbound licence: a letter of cooperation with foreign travel operators and a draft travel-package proposal.
- A premises to operate from.

### Procedure, premises, and TTMC

Apply through TOURLIST with all documents uploaded. Conditional approval is targeted within 5 working days. After conditional approval the company has 6 months to set up premises: minimum 100 sq ft, proper structure, in a commercial zone, supported by a tenancy agreement or S&P, floor plan, photos, and signage. The premises is inspected within 30 working days. At least one company representative must attend the TTMC course and the company must hold a valid TTMC certificate. The licence fee is paid after approval and the licence is printed online. A rejected application can be appealed once, within 14 days.

### Fees and validity

- Processing fee: RM100 per application via TOURLIST.
- Annual licence fee (effective 1 August 2024), main / additional: domestic tour operation RM250 / RM150; outbound tour operation RM500 / RM350; outbound (Umrah) RM500 / RM350; travel agency RM200 / RM100.
- Validity: 1 year. MISHU's internal estimate for the end-to-end process is 3 to 4 months.

### Team-logged nuance — foreign directors and bank-account control

A real MISHU case sharpened two points that the guideline only implies:

- **A foreign director must hold a valid Employment Pass to stay on the board for the application.** On a fully foreign-owned company with one foreign and one local Malaysian director, the foreign director did not yet have an Employment Pass at the time of the MOTAC application. MOTAC would not allow it: the foreign director had to resign, leaving only the local director, for the application to proceed. So before applying, check each foreign director's EP status — no EP means either secure it first or have the foreign director step down and rely on a local director.
- **Bank-account control must sit with the local director.** MOTAC scrutinises the board resolution governing the company bank account, which had to show the local Malaysian director as the controlling signatory. A foreigner cannot be the person in control of the account for the purpose of the application.

Both points reinforce why this licence is tied to immigration work: getting the foreign director's Employment Pass in place first is often the cleaner path than reshuffling the board.

### MISHU service fee

On a worked Klang Valley quotation for an inbound travel agency, MISHU's service fee for the MOTAC licence was RM5,000 (excluding the MOTAC licence fees, which the client bears), bundled with the composite premises licence (RM850) for a total service fee of RM5,850. The government processing fee (RM100), the annual licence fee, and the paid-up-capital requirement are separate.

### Tourist accommodation (PPP) — a separate registration

Hotels and tourist accommodation are registered separately by the Commissioner under section 31C(1) of Act 482, and classified into five categories: City Hotel, Resort Hotel (island/beach/lake/river), Highland Hotel (over 300 m above sea level), Boutique Hotel (unique, personal, luxurious concept), and Innovative Hotel (non-conventional structures — igloo, tree house, container, capsule, and the like). A spa within a tourism setting has its own MOTAC classification guideline. These are distinct from the TOBTAB licence and would each warrant their own detailed pass.

## Related

- [[wrt-licence]] — both regimes gate foreign participation by equity and nationality; a foreign-owned tourism company also needs employment passes for foreign directors, who must additionally show three years of tourism experience here.
- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the council premises licence and commercial-zone requirement overlap with the MOTAC premises conditions; a hotel or resort typically needs both.

## Open Questions

- **Tourist accommodation and spa need their own pass.** Only the classification definitions were captured here; the full PPP registration requirements, the spa classification, and the Umrah/Ziarah add-on guideline are in RAW and warrant dedicated articles.
- **Meeting recording not processed.** "Sharing Session [Sales x License]...Meeting Recording.mp4" in this folder is a video that may hold team nuances; transcribe it separately if needed.
- Confirm whether the three-years-tourism-experience rule applies to the foreign shareholder, the foreign director, or both — the guideline says shareholder; verify against a live case.

articles.title: MPKj composite requirements (Kajang)
articles.summary: Majlis Perbandaran Kajang (MPKj) licenses business premises under its Jabatan Pelesenan & Penjaja (Licensing and Hawker Department) across three licence classes — trade, business, and industrial. The general document set asks for most items in four copies, carries a stated process fee of RM50 for business and RM100 for industrial, and requires a BOMBA fire-safety support letter up front. MPKj's activity table is built around the Hulu Langat district: liquor is licensed by the Hulu Langat District/Land Office and the swiftlet trade needs Hulu Langat PERHILITAN support, which matters when scoping any add-on licences.
articles.body:
# MPKj composite requirements (Kajang)

**Status:** emerging
**Last updated:** 2026-06-21
**Sources:** `RAW/Forms/Majlis Perbandaran Kajang/Panduan permohonan Lesen perniagaan MPKJ.pdf`

## Summary

Majlis Perbandaran Kajang (MPKj) licenses business premises under its Jabatan Pelesenan & Penjaja (Licensing and Hawker Department) across three licence classes — trade, business, and industrial. The general document set asks for most items in four copies, carries a stated process fee of RM50 for business and RM100 for industrial, and requires a BOMBA fire-safety support letter up front. MPKj's activity table is built around the Hulu Langat district: liquor is licensed by the Hulu Langat District/Land Office and the swiftlet trade needs Hulu Langat PERHILITAN support, which matters when scoping any add-on licences.

## Body

### General documents

For the trade/business/industrial licence the applicant supplies: one passport-sized photo; one IC or passport copy (for non-citizens); one company registration certificate (Form M&A and Form 49, or Form A, or Form B plus Form D); one tenancy agreement or proof of building ownership, stamped or endorsed by a Commissioner for Oaths; four copies of the CF (or temporary building permit, or land grant); four copies of the current, paid assessment tax bill; four copies of the business signboard visual (computer-produced); four floor plans; a fire-safety support letter from BOMBA; four business location plans; four front and four rear photos plus one side photo and two interior photos in colour; the process fee (RM50 for business, RM100 for industrial); and confirmation from the Valuation and Property Management Department where needed.

### Activity-specific additions

Food premises need a valid TY2 injection, a food-handling course certificate, and two photos of an installed grease trap. A beauty or health-care centre needs a health-inspection report. A factory needs a production flow chart, a JAS support letter where relevant, and a KDN letter for printing factories only. Used-goods (barang lusuh) trading in an industrial area needs a used-goods licence from the Kajang District Police Headquarters. Childcare, kindergarten and tuition centres need a JKM certificate (childcare), State Education Department approval (kindergarten/tuition), a building-use change approval from residential to commercial issued by the Development Planning Department, and a food-handling certificate plus TY2 where applicable. Liquor needs a licence from the Hulu Langat District/Land Office. Video/VCD needs a FINAS certificate and a KPDNKK support letter. A clinic or medical centre needs a copy of the practitioner's certificate from the Malaysian Medical Association. A council stall (gerai majlis) needs an offer letter from the MPKj Valuation Department and receipts for stall rent, utilities and deposit. The swiftlet (burung walit) trade needs a bird-nest course certificate, Veterinary Department support, and a support letter from PERHILITAN Hulu Langat.

### Team-logged nuances

The MPKj application form is bought and submitted over the counter, needs a real (wet) signature and the company stamp, and the BOMBA support letter is collected from the Hulu Langat branch. Liquor and swiftlet add-ons both route through Hulu Langat district offices rather than the council, so build that lead time into the scope.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the cross-council composite picture; this is the MPKj-specific detail
- [[mps-selayang-composite-requirements]] — closest analogue in document structure
- [[mbsa-composite-requirements]], [[mbsj-composite-requirements]], [[mbpj-composite-requirements]] — other Selangor councils
- [[liquor-licence]] — for MPKj, licensed via the Hulu Langat District/Land Office

## Open Questions

- The guideline names Form M&A / Form 49 / Form A/B/D directly and does not yet mention the SSM MyData CTC printout — confirm whether MPKj now requires it.
- `Borang permohonan MPKJ.pdf`, `MAJLIS PERBANDARAN KAJANG SURAT BOMBA.pdf`, and `CF Form Application (MP KAJANG).pdf` are in RAW but not yet extracted — they likely hold the BOMBA support-letter format and the CF application form.

articles.title: MPS Selayang composite requirements
articles.summary: Majlis Perbandaran Selayang (MPS) issues a composite business-premises-and-signboard licence through its Licensing Department (Persiaran 3, Bandar Baru Selayang, Batu Caves). The MPS checklist runs to seventeen general items, then a long activity-specific table covering 36 business types, plus separate rule sets for vehicle workshops, signboards, and general premises conditions. Two MPS-specific points stand out: the general set asks for photos of a mounted fire extinguisher, a rubbish bin, and a first-aid box, and the signboard rule requires the national language to be 30% larger than any other language.
articles.body:
# MPS Selayang composite requirements

**Status:** emerging
**Last updated:** 2026-06-21
**Sources:** `RAW/Forms/Majlis Perbandaran Selayang/Senarai semak MPS.pdf`

## Summary

Majlis Perbandaran Selayang (MPS) issues a composite business-premises-and-signboard licence through its Licensing Department (Persiaran 3, Bandar Baru Selayang, Batu Caves). The MPS checklist runs to seventeen general items, then a long activity-specific table covering 36 business types, plus separate rule sets for vehicle workshops, signboards, and general premises conditions. Two MPS-specific points stand out: the general set asks for photos of a mounted fire extinguisher, a rubbish bin, and a first-aid box, and the signboard rule requires the national language to be 30% larger than any other language.

## Body

### General documents (17-item checklist)

The SSM portion accepts one of three forms: a Sdn Bhd set (Form 9, the MAA, Form 24, Form 49); an Enterprise or PLT set (Form D, business information, current owner information); or an association/society/club registration certificate. On top of that: a floor plan; a location plan; the tenancy agreement or land grant; an IC or passport copy; a colour advertisement visual approved by DBP; a photo of the advertisement in position; the fire-extinguisher purchase receipt; a photo of the fire extinguisher hung one metre from the floor; the door-tax (cukai pintu) bill or confirmation from the MPS Valuation Department; a photo of the rubbish bin; a photo of the first-aid box; the CF copy or confirmation from the MPS Building Department; an official letter authorising a representative where one is used; and, if no signboard is being applied for, a letter explaining why none is needed.

### Activity-specific additions

The MPS table lists extra documents for 36 activities. Food premises (restaurant, café, food processing and packaging) need the owner's and assistants' typhoid injection, food-handling course certificate, a pest-control contractor appointment, FoSIM registration with the Ministry of Health, and a photo of the installed grease trap. Other notable cases: a money changer needs a Bank Negara licence; a moneylender and a pawnshop need KPKT licences; a foreign-owned hypermarket/supermarket/departmental/specialty store needs KPDNKK distributive-trade approval plus Town Planning approval; used-goods sale needs a Police licence; education-type centres (kindergarten, private school, tuition, language, computer, vocational, skills) need State Education Department approval; a childcare centre needs JKM registration; printing (except inkjet) needs a KDN printing-machine licence; in-building parking needs Town Planning approval, the CCC/CF, and CCTV photos; a hotel needs Town Planning, the CCC/CF, BOMBA approval and pest control; chemical, toxic, flammable, hazardous or scheduled-waste stores need JAS, BOMBA and JKKP approvals; a pharmacy needs Pharmacy Board registration; CD/DVD sale needs FINAS approval; a clinic needs an annual practising certificate; professionals (architect, engineer, lawyer, quantity surveyor, land surveyor, planner) need registration with their professional body; health/beauty centres need typhoid plus a health examination; massage and reflexology add a blood test (HIV, hepatitis, VDRL) and a Police/BOMBA technical review; a cyber café needs pest control, typhoid, and a Police/BOMBA review; a quarry needs Town Planning plus a Forestry or Land Office permit; an entertainment centre needs a Police/BOMBA technical review and pest control; fuel and explosives storage needs BOMBA and JKKP; and a laundry needs Suruhanjaya Tenaga and BOMBA approval.

### Vehicle workshops (by building type)

MPS sets conditions for vehicle repair, servicing, tyre and accessory shops by premises type. A shop lot must rent two parking bays in front of the premises (three for a corner lot) and trade only from 8am to 7pm. A shop office is allowed only on a corner lot, must rent two bays, and trades 8am to 7pm. An industrial-area workshop trades 8am to 8pm, must carry public-liability insurance, and must run weekly pest control with the appointment certificate or receipt shown on inspection. A land lot trades 8am to 8pm, needs neighbour support within a 200-metre radius, must be free of public complaints, and follows the same weekly pest-control rule.

### Signboard rules

A sign or signboard may not be installed before the business licence is obtained. The applicant submits a colour sketch in the set format showing the board size in metres, the activity in the national language, the MPS/licence account number at the bottom right, and whether the sign is lit or unlit; hand-drawn and black-and-white illustrations are not accepted. Projection beyond the shop-lot boundary is capped at 600mm where the sign is at least 2.75m above the road and 1200mm where it is at least 5.00m above the road. A lit board may use only non-blinking lights. The national language must be 30% larger than any other language, given priority in position and grammar, and confirmed by DBP first (dbpsahbahasa.my).

### General premises conditions

Standing conditions cover safety (a complete first-aid box; suitable fire extinguishers approved by BOMBA; adequate lighting and ventilation; well-kept store, stairs and emergency doors; no obstruction of public areas without MPS permission), cleanliness (proper water, electricity and sewerage; "No Smoking" and "Keep Clean" signs displayed; premises kept pest-free; clean toilets; sewage routed to the sewerage system), and waste (a sufficient 240L MBG bin, plastic bags mandatory). The licence cannot be transferred without approval, must be displayed at all times, and can be cancelled by MPS without notice; holders are encouraged to display the royal portraits.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the cross-council composite picture; this is the MPS-specific detail
- [[mbsa-composite-requirements]], [[mbsj-composite-requirements]], [[mbpj-composite-requirements]] — other Selangor councils
- [[mpkj-composite-requirements]] — Kajang, the closest analogue in document structure
- [[liquor-licence]], [[music-licence]], [[halal-certificate]] — industry licences that sit alongside the composite

## Open Questions

- MPS gives the national-language signboard rule as 30% larger; MBSA states 5% larger. Confirm the governing bylaw for each council.
- The checklist predates the SSM MyData era in places (it still names Form 9/24/49 directly) — confirm whether MPS now requires the MyData CTC printout as MBSA and MBSJ do.

articles.title: Music Licence (Public Performance — MACP, PPM, RPM)
articles.summary: Any business that plays recorded music in public in Malaysia — background music in a cafe, a karaoke room, a hotel lobby, a gym, a live band in a bar — needs three separate music licences, not one. Each covers a different copyright held by a different group: MACP for the songwriters and composers, PPM for the record labels that own the sound recording, and RPM for the performers on that recording. All three are collective management organisations (CMOs) declared by MyIPO under the Copyright Act 1987. A premises typically needs a licence from all three to be fully covered. MISHU handles the paperwork, checking, and follow-up for all three bodies as one service line.
articles.body:
# Music Licence (Public Performance — MACP, PPM, RPM)

**Status:** established
**Last updated:** 2026-06-20
**Sources:** `RAW/Music License/QUOTATION/MISHU Business License Services - Composite, Liquor, Music (DBKL).pdf`, `RAW/Music License/QUOTATION/MISHU Business License Services - Music License.docx`, `RAW/Music License/FORMS/Public Performance Malaysia (PPM)/2026 Tariffs [REFER THIS]/PPMB_Standard_Tariffs_R7 (New Rate)_v8 2026.pdf`, `RAW/Music License/FORMS/Recording Performers Malaysia (RPM)/RPM License Tariff_V2_2024.pdf`, `RAW/Music License/FORMS/Public Performance Malaysia (PPM)/PPM Application Form A (Annual Licence) - COMPANY NAME.pdf`, `RAW/Music License/FORMS/Music Authors Copyright Protection (MACP)/MACP Application Form - COMPANY NAME.pdf`

## Summary

Any business that plays recorded music in public in Malaysia — background music in a cafe, a karaoke room, a hotel lobby, a gym, a live band in a bar — needs three separate music licences, not one. Each covers a different copyright held by a different group: MACP for the songwriters and composers, PPM for the record labels that own the sound recording, and RPM for the performers on that recording. All three are collective management organisations (CMOs) declared by MyIPO under the Copyright Act 1987. A premises typically needs a licence from all three to be fully covered. MISHU handles the paperwork, checking, and follow-up for all three bodies as one service line.

## Body

### Why three licences, not one

When a song plays in public, several distinct rights are in use at once, and Malaysian copyright law assigns each to a different rights-holder group represented by a different CMO:

- **MACP — Music Authors' Copyright Protection Berhad** (186403-X) covers the musical work itself: the composition and lyrics, owned by authors and composers. Office at Unit 8, Level U2, Block D4, Solaris Dutamas, Kuala Lumpur. Tel 03-6207 8638.
- **PPM — Public Performance Malaysia Berhad** (201601042369) covers the sound recording — the actual recorded track, owned and controlled by record companies (PPM members). A not-for-profit CMO declared by MyIPO under the Copyright Act 1987 (Act 332). Office at L-8-2, No. 2, Jalan Solaris, Solaris Mont' Kiara, Kuala Lumpur. Tel 03-6207 2888.
- **RPM — Recording Performers Malaysia Berhad** (201101036650 / 964784-U) covers the performers' right to equitable remuneration. This is a statutory right under Section 16B(1) of the Copyright Act 1987: users of commercial sound recordings must pay performers equitable remuneration for the public performance of those recordings. Office at Unit 15-09, Office Suites, One Mont Kiara, Kuala Lumpur. Tel 03-6411 8336.

A business that holds only one of the three is still exposed on the other two. For full coverage the team scopes all three together.

### Featured vs non-featured entertainment

This distinction runs through all three tariff schedules and drives which rate applies:

- **Featured entertainment** — music is the main feature: live bands, DJs, dancing, karaoke singing, product launches, entertainment by disc jockeys. Higher tariffs.
- **Non-featured entertainment** — music is background only: piped-in music, radio or TV broadcasts in a shop, lobby, or restaurant. Lower tariffs.

A premises offering non-featured entertainment that later runs a featured event (e.g. a restaurant hosting a live act) needs separate featured-entertainment cover for that event (PPM terms, clause 18).

### How the licence works (common to all three)

- **Validity:** 12 months from the commencement date (the day music usage started), renewed automatically unless cancelled in writing.
- **Supporting documents:** the application must be backed by the company's business registration (SSM) and/or the premises licence issued by the local council. MACP requires this explicitly; PPM requires supporting documents for verification and reserves the right to refuse incomplete applications.
- **Signatory:** an authorised person, director, or business owner of the company signs; multiple premises are listed on an attached schedule/appendix.
- **Fee basis:** calculated from the published standard tariff according to business category, capacity or floor area, and surcharges. PPM invoices are payable in full within 14 days; late payment carries 1% interest per month.
- **Right of entry:** the CMO may inspect the premises during business hours to verify the details the fee is based on.
- **Material changes:** the licence holder must notify the CMO within 14 days of any change of premises, address, or how music is used.

### Tariff structure — how fees are set

Each CMO publishes a standard tariff with the business categorised by type. Fees scale with capacity (persons or seats), floor area (sq ft), or room/unit count, plus per-item surcharges for TV monitors, video walls/multiple screens, and stand-alone karaoke units. Karaoke that reproduces digital copies attracts an additional reproduction tariff (PPM publishes a separate "Reproduction for Public Performance — Karaoke Recording" schedule).

Indicative figures for common F&B categories (rates change yearly — always check the current schedule):

- **PPM (effective 1 May 2026 – 30 Apr 2027), restaurant with pub or bar facility, non-featured:** RM1,520.70 for the first 50 persons, RM12.17 each additional; featured rates run far higher (a discotheque/nightclub/pub/bar at PP02 starts at RM4,055.20 for up to 50 persons). Eatery/coffee house/cafe/food court (PP04, non-featured): RM729.94 for the first 50 persons, RM3.04 each additional. Traditional coffee shop (PP05): RM304.14 first TV/audio player. Monitor surcharge RM202.76; video wall RM1,013.80; stand-alone karaoke RM1,013.80.
- **RPM (effective 1 October 2024), restaurant/cafe/coffee house/gaming centre (category 7):** featured RM650 first 50 seats + RM6.50 each additional; non-featured RM400 first 50 seats + RM4.00 each additional. Pub/bar/bistro/lounge (category 6): featured RM1,600 first 50 + RM16.00 each additional. Surcharges RM100/monitor, RM500/video wall, RM500/stand-alone karaoke.
- **RPM single-event rates** exist for one-off functions: wedding function RM200 flat, dinner & dance / product launch RM300 first 100 persons + RM1.50 each additional, exhibition/seminar/conference RM350 flat, concert RM500 first 1,000 persons (capped at RM5,000), blanket annual one-off licence RM20,000.

MACP publishes its own tariff schedule keyed to the same categories (its application form groups premises into the same business types — executive KTV/karaoke, pub/bar/bistro, restaurant/cafe/food court, retail/office/factory, and so on).

All tariffs are subject to yearly CPI adjustment and change without notice; the CMO decides the final categorisation of any establishment.

### MISHU service scope and fee

MISHU's service covers, for each of the three bodies: paperwork and document processing, checking, and follow-up to obtain the licence certificate. On the worked DBKL quotation (composite + liquor + music, 5 Jun 2022), the music licence line — all three of MACP, PPM and RPM together — was priced at **RM700** service fee. This is the professional fee only; the licence fees payable to MACP, PPM and RPM (per their tariffs above) are borne by the client, as are SST and any agency charges.

The music licence usually appears as an add-on alongside the F&B composite licence and (where alcohol is served) the liquor licence, since the same premises commonly needs all three.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — F&B premises commonly need all three music licences on top of the composite and (if serving alcohol) liquor licence.
- [[liquor-licence]] — frequently scoped together with music for restaurants, pubs and bars.

## Open Questions

- The standalone "MISHU Business License Services - Music License.docx" quotation is now extracted (5 Jan 2022, MBPJ): it confirms the music line (MACP + PPM + RPM together) at RM700, within a wider bundle (composite RM1,000, health/premise food RM700, fire certificate RM1,200 optional; total MISHU service fee RM3,600). The RM700 music figure is consistent across both the DBKL and MBPJ quotations.
- MACP's full standard tariff schedule (rates per category) is not yet in RAW — only the application form and licence-agreement terms are. The application form confirms the category groupings but not the RM amounts. Obtain MACP's current tariff card to complete the fee picture.
- Confirm whether all three bodies are always required for every premises, or whether some low-music-use categories (e.g. background radio only) are exempt from one or more. The team's practical default appears to be "apply to all three" — worth logging a nuance if exceptions arise.
- RPM tariff in RAW is the 2024 edition (effective 1 Oct 2024); check for a newer schedule before quoting.

articles.body:
# WRT & USS Licences (foreign-owned trade and services)

**Status:** established
**Last updated:** 2026-06-20
**Sources:** `RAW/Wholesale, Retail & Trading (WRT) License/CHECKLIST/WRT_Guideline.pdf`, `RAW/Wholesale, Retail & Trading (WRT) License/CHECKLIST/senarai_semak_permohonan_baru_dan_pembaharuan_menjalankan_perdagangan_pengedaran_borang__runcit_wrt-converted.pdf`, `RAW/Wholesale, Retail & Trading (WRT) License/FORMS/FORMAT BORANG USS (1).doc`, `RAW/Wholesale, Retail & Trading (WRT) License/QUOTATION/WRT License.docx`, `RAW/Wholesale, Retail & Trading (WRT) License/uss-wrt-web-digest.md`

## Summary

WRT and USS are sibling licences from the same ministry (KPDN, formerly MDTCC), both for companies with foreign (non-Malaysian) shareholding. WRT — Wholesale, Retail & Trade — covers foreign-owned businesses trading in goods. USS — Unregulated Services Sector — covers foreign-owned businesses providing services that aren't otherwise regulated. The rule of thumb: if a foreign-owned company sells goods it needs WRT; if it provides services it needs USS; if it does both, it needs one licence chosen by where most of its revenue comes from. Because the two cover the field between them, **whenever a consultant scopes a business type for a client with any foreign shareholding, check both** — applying for the wrong one means rejection and a restart of the two-to-three-month process. For MISHU this sits close to our immigration work: a foreign-owned company generally needs its WRT or USS approval in place before it can apply for Employment Passes for expatriate staff. The pre-screening job is to confirm whether the activity is goods (WRT) or services (USS), check the minimum paid-up capital, and flag activities that foreigners simply aren't allowed to do.

## Body

### WRT or USS? Check both first

Both licences apply only to companies with foreign shareholding, and between them they cover almost any foreign-owned business. Decide which one before anything else:

- **WRT** — the company trades in goods (wholesale, retail, distribution, import/export).
- **USS** — the company provides services that aren't separately regulated (see the USS section below for the service categories).
- **Both activities** — only one licence is needed, chosen by where the majority of revenue comes from.

The two share the same ministry (KPDN), the same WRT 1 form, the same document checklist, the same BLESS submission, the same two-to-three-month processing time, and the same one-to-three-year validity. The practical risk is picking the wrong one: KPDN rejects a misfiled application, and the client restarts the whole process. USS is the less well-known of the pair, so it's the one that gets missed. When a consultant is asked about a business type for a foreign-shareholding client, run both past the activity before scoping.

### Who needs it and who grants it

The licence applies to foreign business operators engaged in distributive trade. Under the guideline, "foreign participation" means a non-citizen individual (including permanent residents), a foreign company or institution, or a local company in which foreigners hold more than 50% of the voting rights. With effect from 6 January 2010, all proposals for foreign involvement in distributive trade need KPDN/MDTCC approval — this covers acquiring an interest, mergers and takeovers, opening, relocating, or expanding branches and outlets, and buying property to run distributive trade.

KPDN is the secretariat to the Committee on Distributive Trade, which is the reference point for all applications. A purely Malaysian-owned company does not need a WRT licence — this is a foreign-equity requirement.

### What counts as distributive trade

Distributive trade is the chain of activities that channel goods and services to intermediaries for resale or to final buyers. It captures wholesalers, retailers, franchisors and franchisees, direct sellers, suppliers, and commission agents or representatives of international trading companies.

- **Wholesale trade** — sale or resale of goods and services to other distributors buying for their business.
- **Retail trade** — sale or resale of goods and services to the general public for final consumption.

It does not include manufacturing companies, or companies granted MIDA regional-establishment status (International Procurement Centres, Regional Distribution Centres, Operational Headquarters). Products governed by other Acts — petroleum, pharmaceuticals, toxic substances, explosives, arms, agricultural raw materials, live animals — sit under their own regimes.

### Business formats and minimum capital (the pre-screening table)

Minimum capital here means shareholders' funds (paid-up capital plus reserves). The format determines the threshold, so this is the first thing to check with a foreign-investor client:

- **Hypermarket** — sales floor 5,000 sqm or more; minimum RM50 million; must reserve at least 30% equity for Bumiputera; standalone, on the outskirts of major towns; one allowed per 250,000 residents; not within a 3.5 km radius of residential areas or town centres.
- **Superstore** — 3,000 to under 4,999 sqm; minimum RM25 million; only companies already operating a hypermarket may apply; one per 200,000 residents.
- **Departmental store** — minimum RM20 million (reviewed every 3 years); may include a supermarket of not more than 2,000 sqm.
- **Specialty store** — one main brand or product line (furniture, electrical, healthcare, optical, footwear, apparel, sports, books, jewellery, electronics, pharmacy, and similar); minimum RM1 million per outlet.
- **Various other distribution formats** — considered case by case on socio-economic merit; minimum RM1 million per outlet.
- **Franchisor / franchisee** — governed by the Franchise Act 1998; minimum capital assessed case by case.

All foreign-equity distributive trade companies must be incorporated locally under the Companies Act. A foreign branch has not been allowed to carry on wholesale or retail trade in Malaysia since 1 November 1995.

### Standing conditions for foreign-equity companies

Every distributive trade company with foreign equity must: appoint Bumiputera director(s); build a workforce reflecting Malaysia's racial composition at all levels including management; have clear plans to assist Bumiputera participation; hire at least 1% of the hypermarket workforce from persons with disabilities; use local airports and ports for import and export; use local legal and professional services; submit annual financial reports to KPDN; and comply with local authority by-laws. Larger-format stores also carry a commitment to allocate at least 30% of shelf space (SKUs) to Bumiputera SME products within three years.

### What foreigners are not allowed to do

The guideline closes several activities to foreign involvement entirely. Flag these early — they are not licensable for a foreign-owned client:

- Supermarket or mini-market under 3,000 sqm; provision shops and general vendors; 24-hour convenience stores; news agents; medical halls; fuel stations (with or without a convenience store); permanent wet-market and pavement stores; textiles; non-exclusive restaurants; bistros; and jewellery shops.

Note the restaurant rule in particular: the Ministry only approves an **exclusive** restaurant for foreign equity. To qualify as exclusive, the restaurant must be at least one shop lot (1,000–1,500 sq ft), be air-conditioned, have a unique and specialised menu, have decor in line with that menu, and meet cleanliness and location standards. An ordinary restaurant cannot be foreign-owned via WRT.

### USS — the services-sector sibling

USS (Unregulated Services Sector) is what a foreign-owned company applies for when its business is providing services rather than trading goods. It is issued by the same ministry (KPDN) and exists for the same reason as WRT: to manage foreign participation while protecting local businesses. A foreign-owned company that doesn't fall under WRT will usually fall under USS instead — so the two should always be checked together.

Service categories that typically need USS:

- Transport and logistics.
- Real estate and property management.
- Equipment leasing and maintenance.
- Professional, business, and research services (consultancies, training, technical services, and similar).
- Community and personal care services.

(KPDN publishes an official list of USS-covered services; obtain and ingest it for the definitive scope before advising on a borderline case.)

General requirements for USS mirror WRT closely: the company must be incorporated under the Companies Act 2016, hold a minimum paid-up capital of RM1 million, and already have its local council (PBT) premises and signboard approvals in place. The application uses the same WRT 1 form, the same document checklist, and the same BLESS submission described below.

The official USS application form confirms the framework: titled "Application Form for the Establishment of Distributive Trade in Malaysia — Unregulated Services Sector," it sits under the same distributive-trade regime as WRT and collects the same picture of the company — establishment details and MyCoID, the USS trade type, headquarters or branch, sales floor area, parent company, share capital (authorised and issued/paid-up), the equity structure split across Bumiputera, non-Bumiputera, and foreign holders, fixed assets, investment, and the employment structure. The service-category list above still rests on secondary sources, so confirm a borderline activity against KPDN's official USS service list before advising.

### Exemption: make the company majority Malaysian

Because both WRT and USS apply only to foreign-shareholding companies, a company with at least 51% Malaysian ownership may be exempted from the licence entirely — and from the RM1 million paid-up-capital requirement that comes with it. This is not guaranteed and comes down to the discretion of the officer handling the case, but it's a route worth raising with a client who is flexible on ownership structure.

### Application and documents

Applications are made on Form WRT 1, submitted to the Committee on Distributive Trade at KPDN — in practice through the BLESS online system. Only complete submissions are processed, and renewals must be filed at least three months before expiry. The checklist (last updated 18 July 2018) calls for:

- The completed WRT 1 form, plus a company profile / business plan and a detailed colour product catalogue or brochure.
- SSM records: latest ROC computer print-out (e-Info) and Form 49 / Section 14 (particulars of directors) certified by SSM; stamped Form 32A if shareholding has changed.
- LHDN tax registration confirmation (new company) or latest e-C acknowledgement (operating company).
- EPF registration proof (new) or the latest three months' contribution statement (operating).
- A valid local authority (PBT) business licence — or, if the company has none yet, a stamped tenancy agreement or sale-and-purchase agreement for the premises.
- Audited financial statements for the last three years, with a profit/loss justification.
- Any product approval letters where relevant: PBKD for medicines/health food, Suruhanjaya Tenaga or SIRIM for electrical/energy products, NPRA for cosmetics.
- Three sample transactions per month for the last three months: Customs documents (K1/K2), airway bill or bill of lading, DO/PO, and sales invoices.
- Foreign worker / expatriate details: names and valid employment pass or passport copies (with position, salary, nationality, job description), or an estimate of expatriates to be hired.
- For an exclusive restaurant: the menu and prices, plus the chef's academic certificate or proof of at least three years' culinary experience.
- Colour photographs of the premises, warehouse, office, and product samples.

### Why this matters for MISHU's immigration work

The expatriate section of the checklist is the tell: WRT/USS and Employment Pass applications are linked. A foreign-owned company generally needs its WRT or USS approval before KDN/Immigration will entertain Employment Passes for its expatriate staff, because that approval is what establishes the company is licensed to operate. When scoping a foreign-investor client who wants to bring in staff, check whether a WRT or USS licence is needed first — see [[fnb-composite-licence]] for the council-level premises licence that also feeds the document set. Note KPDN does not charge an application fee for either WRT or USS; the cost to the client is the professional service fee plus the RM1 million paid-up-capital requirement.

### MISHU service fee

On a worked 2022 quotation, MISHU charged RM6,000 (all-inclusive, excluding SST) to handle a WRT application for a trading company end to end — KPDN paperwork and processing, checking with the relevant departments, follow-up, and delivery of the licence — with an estimated completion of three to six months. The same scope and fee basis apply to a USS application.

## Related

- [[fnb-composite-licence]] — the PBT premises licence is part of the WRT document set; an exclusive foreign-owned restaurant needs both.
- [[liquor-licence]] — foreign-owned outlets trading in alcohol face both the WRT activity rules and the separate liquor licence.

## Open Questions

- **USS official service list still pending.** The USS application form is now ingested (primary source confirming the framework), but KPDN's official list of USS-covered service categories is not yet in RAW — the service categories above still rest on secondary sources. Obtain that list to put the USS scope on a fully primary footing.
- **Confirm the current administering ministry name and capital thresholds.** The guideline in RAW predates the rename from MDTCC to KPDN and may predate threshold revisions (several figures are marked "review every 3 years"). Verify against the current KPDN guideline before quoting capital requirements to a client.
- **WRT-before-EP sequencing.** The link between WRT approval and Employment Pass eligibility is inferred from the checklist's expatriate section and MISHU's practice. Log a real-case confirmation of the exact sequence as a team nuance, and cross-link to the EP/immigration material once that is in the KB.
- Not yet ingested in this category: the WRT 1 form itself, the older 2012 checklist/CPC list, the USS form, and the tax justification letter template.
